writeup.title: Sheet kinetic inductance in NbSe2 thin films decreases strongly and monotonically with increasing film thickness, consistent with an approximate inverse power-law relationship.
writeup.hypothesis: In NbSe2 thin films, sheet kinetic inductance (Lk,sq) decreases monotonically with increasing film thickness, following an approximate inverse power-law (1/d) relationship.
writeup.summary: Across all 12 NbSe2 samples from a single study (Zaman_2025), Lk,sq spans nearly three orders of magnitude — from 1195 pH/sq at 1.99 nm to 4 pH/sq at 52.12 nm — with no exceptions to the monotonic ordering. The trend is physically well-motivated by both the geometric electrodynamic relationship (Lk,sq ∝ λL²/d) and disorder-driven enhancement of the London penetration depth in the dirty limit at low thickness. However, because all samples originate from a single study on a single substrate, independent replication is required before this relationship can be treated as a general material property of NbSe2.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: All 12 samples follow a perfectly monotonic negative relationship between thickness and Lk,sq, spanning nearly three orders of magnitude in Lk,sq (4–1195 pH/sq) over a thickness range of ~2–52 nm; a log-log plot yields a near-linear trend consistent with an approximate 1/d power law, and the dirty-to-clean transport regime transition aligns coherently with the Lk,sq suppression.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Samples D6 (8.81 nm, 124 pH/sq) and D7 (8.90 nm, 107 pH/sq) differ by only 0.09 nm in thickness yet show a ~15% difference in Lk,sq, suggesting sample-to-sample disorder variability at similar nominal thicknesses; additionally, the abrupt drop in Lk,sq between D7 and D8 (~107 to 56 pH/sq over ~1.2 nm) coincides with a transport regime reclassification, raising the possibility that the 1/d relationship is not smoothly continuous across the dirty-to-clean crossover.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Independent replication of the thickness–Lk,sq trend in NbSe2 by a different group or on a different substrate, combined with direct measurements of London penetration depth (λL) as a function of thickness, to decouple the geometric 1/d contribution from disorder-driven λL enhancement.

writeup.finding_detail: The dataset exhibits a remarkably clean inverse relationship between film thickness and sheet kinetic inductance across all 12 NbSe2 samples. The thinnest sample (D1: 1.99 nm, 1195 pH/sq) and thickest sample (D12: 52.12 nm, 4 pH/sq) anchor a dynamic range of ~300×, and intermediate samples (e.g., D2: 4.08 nm / 282 pH/sq; D3: 5.86 nm / 191 pH/sq; D10: 24.73 nm / 8 pH/sq) fall consistently along the trend. Importantly, part of this correlation is partially definitional: the standard electrodynamic expression Lk,sq = μ₀λL²/d guarantees a 1/d dependence even at fixed λL, so the geometric factor alone would produce a strong inverse trend. The observed relationship appears steeper than pure 1/d at the thinnest samples (D1–D2), which is consistent with dirty-limit enhancement of λL due to surface scattering and reduced mean free path, and possibly with charge-density-wave competition or proximity to the superconductor-insulator transition in the sub-5 nm regime. The regime transition near 10 nm (D7→D8) introduces a localized non-linearity that warrants scrutiny, but does not break the overall monotonic ordering. All samples derive from a single source (Zaman_2025), which removes inter-lab confounders but also means the finding lacks independent corroboration.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The dominant alternative is that the observed trend is substantially geometric in origin — Lk,sq ∝ λL²/d guarantees an inverse thickness dependence even if λL is constant, meaning the correlation is partially definitional rather than purely empirical. A secondary alternative is that the steepening at low thickness reflects CDW-driven suppression of superfluid density or proximity to the superconductor-insulator transition, rather than (or in addition to) simple dirty-limit disorder enhancement of λL.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.75. The monotonic ordering across all 12 samples with a ~300× dynamic range in Lk,sq is a strong empirical signal that is unlikely to be attributable to measurement noise, and the trend is supported by well-established electrodynamic theory. However, three factors prevent a higher confidence rating: (1) all 12 samples originate from a single study (Zaman_2025) on a single substrate (high-resistivity Si), so the finding has not been independently replicated; (2) the geometric 1/d factor means a portion of the correlation is partially definitional, making it difficult to isolate the material-intrinsic component without λL measurements; and (3) the D6/D7 variability and the abrupt D7→D8 regime transition introduce local deviations from a smooth power law that are not yet explained.
writeup.qrem_implications: This finding has direct and practically significant implications for the QREM materials-to-device mapping layer. A mapping function of the form Lk,sq(d) ≈ A · d^(−α) is empirically supported for NbSe2 on high-resistivity Si, with α ≈ 1 as a first approximation (consistent with geometric 1/d scaling) and potentially α > 1 at sub-5 nm thicknesses due to dirty-limit λL enhancement. For QREM purposes, film thickness can be treated as a primary tuning parameter for kinetic inductance in NbSe2-based kinetic inductance detectors (KIDs) or superinductance elements, with the caveat that the mapping function may not be a simple power law across the full thickness range — particularly near the dirty-to-clean crossover (~8–10 nm) and near the superconductor-insulator transition (<3 nm). The mapping function should be parameterized separately for the dirty-limit (d < ~8 nm) and clean-limit (d > ~10 nm) regimes. Until independent replication on other substrates is available, the mapping function should be flagged as single-source and substrate-conditional in the QREM layer.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: This hypothesis should be re-run per material class as additional data become available. Currently, only NbSe2 has sufficient samples (n=12) to warrant a focused re-analysis, and that re-analysis should explicitly test whether a two-regime power law (dirty-limit vs. clean-limit) fits better than a single power law. No other 2D superconductor in the corpus currently has comparable thickness-resolved Lk,sq data; NbN and TaS2 are priority candidates for future data collection to test whether the 1/d relationship is universal among 2D superconductors.
questions_for_reviewer: Given that Lk,sq ∝ λL²/d is a geometric identity, how much of the observed ~300× dynamic range in Lk,sq is attributable to the 1/d factor alone versus disorder-driven changes in λL? Is there a way to estimate the λL(d) dependence from the existing data (e.g., using the ratio Lk,sq · d as a proxy for λL²) to decouple these contributions?

The abrupt drop in Lk,sq between D7 (8.90 nm, 107 pH/sq) and D8 (10.07 nm, 56 pH/sq) coincides with a transport regime reclassification from 'intermediate' to 'clean_limit'. Is this regime boundary physically sharp (e.g., a crossover length scale in NbSe2) or is it an artifact of the classification criteria used by Zaman_2025? Would you expect a smooth or discontinuous transition in Lk,sq at this thickness?

NbSe2 hosts a charge-density-wave (CDW) phase that is known to strengthen at lower thickness. At what thickness does CDW order become significant enough to materially affect superfluid density and hence Lk,sq in this system, and do the Zaman_2025 samples show any signatures (e.g., in Tc suppression or residual resistance) that CDW competition is contributing to the elevated Lk,sq at D1–D2?

For QREM device mapping: at what Lk,sq value (and corresponding thickness) does NbSe2 become practically useful as a superinductance element for qubit circuits, and does the high disorder in the sub-5 nm regime (D1–D2) introduce loss mechanisms (e.g., two-level systems, vortex dissipation) that would degrade qubit coherence despite the high kinetic inductance?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-priority follow-up is a systematic measurement of London penetration depth (λL) or superfluid density (n_s) as a function of thickness across the same 12 NbSe2 samples (or a comparable set), which would allow direct decomposition of the Lk,sq trend into its geometric (1/d) and material (λL(d)) components. Concurrently, replication of the thickness–Lk,sq measurement on NbSe2 films deposited on a different substrate (e.g., sapphire or SiO2) by an independent group would establish whether the mapping function is substrate-independent and thus suitable for general inclusion in the QREM layer.

writeup.title: Sheet kinetic inductance in NbSe2 thin films decreases monotonically with film thickness over a ~300-fold dynamic range, consistent with an approximate inverse power law.
writeup.hypothesis: In NbSe2 thin films, sheet kinetic inductance (Lk,sq) decreases with increasing film thickness following an approximate 1/d power law, with thinner films exhibiting dramatically higher kinetic inductance due to dirty-limit transport behavior.
writeup.summary: Across all 12 NbSe2 samples from a single study (Zaman_2025), sheet kinetic inductance decreases monotonically from 1195 pH/sq at 1.99 nm to 4 pH/sq at 52.12 nm — a ~300-fold range — with no rank-order reversals. The trend is consistent with an approximate inverse power law and co-varies with transport regime classification (dirty → intermediate → clean limit), providing mechanistic corroboration. However, all samples originate from a single laboratory and material system, and the causal mechanism (geometric 1/d scaling vs. disorder-driven superfluid density suppression) remains ambiguous.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: All 12 samples exhibit a perfectly monotonic rank ordering of Lk,sq with thickness across a ~300-fold dynamic range (1195 pH/sq at 1.99 nm to 4 pH/sq at 52.12 nm), with transport regime labels (dirty, intermediate, clean) co-varying monotonically with thickness — providing both empirical and mechanistic support for the hypothesis.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Samples D6 (8.81 nm, 124 pH/sq) and D7 (8.90 nm, 107 pH/sq) differ by only 0.09 nm in thickness but by ~16% in Lk,sq, suggesting that sub-nm thickness measurement uncertainty or local disorder fluctuations near the dirty-to-clean crossover may contribute non-trivial scatter that the simple 1/d model does not capture.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Explicit sheet resistance (Rn,sq) measurements at each thickness are absent, making it impossible to determine whether Lk,sq correlates more tightly with Rn,sq than with d directly — a test that would distinguish geometric 1/d scaling from disorder-driven superfluid density suppression as the dominant mechanism.

writeup.finding_detail: The 12 NbSe2 samples span a 26-fold range in thickness (1.99–52.12 nm) and a ~300-fold range in Lk,sq (4–1195 pH/sq), with no reversals in rank ordering. The relationship is clearly super-linear on a linear scale and visually consistent with a 1/d power law. The thinnest samples (D1 at 1.99 nm, D2 at 4.08 nm) are classified as dirty-limit and anchor the high-Lk,sq extreme; the thickest samples (D10–D12, 24–52 nm) are clean-limit and anchor the low-Lk,sq extreme. The intermediate samples (D3–D7, 5.86–8.90 nm) bridge these regimes smoothly. A minor anomaly exists between D6 (8.81 nm, 124 pH/sq) and D7 (8.90 nm, 107 pH/sq), where a 0.09 nm thickness difference corresponds to a ~16% Lk,sq difference — possibly reflecting heightened sensitivity to disorder near the dirty-to-clean crossover, or sub-nm thickness measurement imprecision. Critically, all 12 samples derive from a single study and substrate (high-resistivity Si), so the finding is well-established within this dataset but cannot yet be generalized to other NbSe2 sources, substrates, or superconducting material families.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that the 1/d trend is a secondary consequence of disorder scaling rather than a geometric effect: thinner NbSe2 films have higher normal-state sheet resistance (Rn,sq) and shorter mean free paths, which independently elevate Lk,sq through the dirty-limit BCS relation Lk,sq ∝ Rn,sq/Δ. Additionally, quantum confinement, surface oxidation, and substrate-induced disorder in ultra-thin films (1–4 nm) could suppress superfluid density beyond what pure geometric arguments predict, making the simple 1/d description an approximation that conflates multiple co-varying physical effects.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is 0.72, reflecting strong internal consistency within the dataset (perfect monotonic ordering, enormous dynamic range, mechanistic corroboration from transport regime labels, and direct author support) offset by significant limitations: all 12 samples originate from a single study and a single material system (NbSe2), introducing strong single-source bias; the causal mechanism is unresolved without Rn,sq data; no cross-material or cross-laboratory validation exists in the corpus; and the D6/D7 anomaly hints at sub-nm thickness uncertainty. The finding is robustly supported within NbSe2 from Zaman_2025 but should be treated as material- and source-specific pending broader evidence.
writeup.qrem_implications: This finding provides meaningful support for a materials-to-device mapping function of the form Lk,sq(d) ≈ A/d^α for NbSe2, where α is approximately 1 (inverse power law) and A is a material- and disorder-dependent prefactor. Within the NbSe2 dataset, such a mapping function appears practically usable for circuit design in the thickness range 2–52 nm, with Lk,sq predictable to within the scatter observed near the dirty-to-clean crossover (~10–20%). However, the mapping function cannot yet be stated as universal: the prefactor A likely encodes disorder and substrate effects that vary with fabrication conditions, and the exponent α may differ between the dirty-limit and clean-limit regimes (i.e., the function may be piecewise rather than a single power law). For QREM purposes, a two-regime mapping (dirty-limit: α ≈ 1 with high A; clean-limit: α ≈ 1 with lower A) would be more physically honest than a single global fit. Extension to other superconductors (NbN, TiN, MoRe) requires new data before a general mapping layer can be constructed.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Yes — this hypothesis should be re-run per material class as additional data become available. Currently, only NbSe2 has sufficient samples (n=12) to warrant a focused re-analysis, and that re-analysis should include a quantitative power-law fit with uncertainty bounds and a test of whether Rn,sq or d is the better predictor of Lk,sq. No other material in the 154-sample corpus has enough samples with both film_thickness_nm and kinetic_inductance_sheet_pH_sq measured to support a comparable analysis. NbN and TiN are the highest-priority targets for future data collection given their established use in kinetic inductance devices.
questions_for_reviewer: The D6/D7 pair (8.81 nm vs. 8.90 nm, 124 vs. 107 pH/sq) shows a ~16% Lk,sq difference for a 0.09 nm thickness difference. Is this level of thickness measurement precision (sub-nm via, e.g., AFM or ellipsometry) routinely achievable in NbSe2 exfoliated or transferred films, or is it likely that the true thickness uncertainty is larger than reported, making this pair effectively indistinguishable in thickness?

The transport regime labels (dirty_limit, intermediate, clean_limit) co-vary with thickness, but the physical criterion used to assign these labels is not explicit in the corpus. Were these assignments made based on measured mean free path vs. coherence length, or inferred from Lk,sq values themselves? If the latter, the mechanistic corroboration argument is partially circular and should be flagged.

Is there a physical reason to expect the 1/d power law exponent to remain constant across the dirty-to-clean transition in NbSe2, or should the QREM mapping function use a piecewise model with different exponents in each regime? Do the authors of Zaman_2025 report a fitted exponent, and if so, does it deviate significantly from α=1?

All samples are on high-resistivity Si substrates. Are there known substrate-induced proximity or disorder effects in NbSe2 on Si that would make the Lk,sq values non-transferable to other substrate choices (e.g., sapphire, SiO2, hBN encapsulation) relevant to quantum circuit fabrication?
writeup.suggested_followup: The single most valuable next analysis would be to obtain or request explicit normal-state sheet resistance (Rn,sq) values for each of the 12 Zaman_2025 samples and perform a comparative regression of Lk,sq against both 1/d and Rn,sq. If Lk,sq correlates more tightly with Rn,sq than with 1/d, the disorder-driven mechanism dominates and the QREM mapping function should be parameterized by Rn,sq rather than d. If 1/d is the better predictor after controlling for Rn,sq, the geometric interpretation is strengthened. This analysis requires no new experiments — only extraction of already-measured quantities from the source paper — and would directly resolve the primary mechanistic ambiguity identified in this finding.

writeup.title: Dirty-limit Ta thin films exhibit dramatically elevated vortex activation temperatures relative to clean-limit films, with a regime-crossing effect dominating over a smooth monotonic relationship.
writeup.hypothesis: In Ta thin films, longer mean free path (cleaner, less disordered films) is negatively correlated with vortex activation temperature, such that dirty-limit films with short mean free paths exhibit substantially higher vortex activation temperatures.
writeup.summary: Across 8 Ta thin film samples from a single study (Bahrami_2026), dirty-limit samples (mean free path l ≈ 13–20 nm) show vortex activation temperatures of 1.69–4.17 K, roughly 7–14× higher than clean-limit samples (l ≈ 82–142 nm, Tact = 0.29–0.57 K), strongly supporting the proposed negative correlation. However, the relationship appears regime-dependent rather than smoothly monotonic: within the clean-limit subgroup, the ordering of Tact with l is non-monotonic, suggesting the dominant physical effect is the clean/dirty-limit boundary crossing rather than a continuous l–Tact function. Generalizability is severely constrained by single-study, single-material provenance and n = 8.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: Two near-replicate dirty-limit samples (D7: l=12.9 nm, Tact=4.16 K; D8: l=13.3 nm, Tact=4.17 K) provide a robust anchor at the dirty extreme, and an intermediate sample (D6: l=20.4 nm, Tact=1.69 K) forms a coherent trend consistent with the negative correlation across the clean/dirty-limit boundary.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Within the clean-limit subgroup (l ≈ 82–142 nm), the Tact ordering is non-monotonic — D3 (l=81.5 nm) has the lowest Tact (0.29 K) while D5 (l=112.2 nm) has the highest (0.57 K) — indicating that mean free path alone does not reliably predict Tact once in the clean limit, and that the overall correlation is dominated by the regime-crossing effect.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Crystal phase identification (XRD for α-Ta vs. β-Ta fraction) per sample is the most critical missing data, as β-Ta co-varies with short mean free path and has distinct vortex dynamics; without this, the correlation cannot be attributed to l per se rather than to phase purity.

writeup.finding_detail: The 8 samples partition naturally into two groups: a dirty-limit cluster (D6, D7, D8; l = 12.9–20.4 nm; Tact = 1.69–4.17 K) and a clean-limit cluster (D1–D5; l = 81.5–142.3 nm; Tact = 0.29–0.57 K), with no samples in the intermediate range (l ≈ 20–80 nm). The dirty-limit anchor is robustly established by the near-replicate pair D7/D8, which differ by only 0.4 nm in l and 0.01 K in Tact, confirming this is a real physical effect rather than a measurement outlier. Within the clean-limit group, D3 (l=81.5 nm, Tact=0.29 K) and D4 (l=125.2 nm, Tact=0.33 K) cluster at low Tact, while D1 (l=109.3 nm, Tact=0.54 K) and D5 (l=112.2 nm, Tact=0.57 K) are elevated despite longer mean free paths, a non-monotonicity that likely reflects film-to-film variation in surface/interface quality or grain boundary density not captured by bulk transport-derived l. Importantly, mean_free_path_nm is almost certainly derived from resistivity measurements via the ρ·l product for Ta, meaning the correlation may partly reflect a ρ–Tact relationship with l as an intermediate quantity; however, since Tact is extracted independently from magnetotransport data, circular derivation is not a concern, though both quantities may share a common upstream processing variable (e.g., sputtering pressure).
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that the observed correlation is driven by crystal phase co-variation: β-Ta (higher resistivity, shorter l) has a different vortex pinning energy landscape than α-Ta, and without XRD confirmation of phase fraction per sample, the l–Tact correlation cannot be disentangled from a phase-purity effect. A secondary alternative is that shorter l implies greater structural disorder and thus higher vortex pinning site density, making the mechanism disorder-enhanced pinning rather than a direct consequence of mean free path.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.72 reflecting a clear, well-supported directional pattern with a large effect size (~7–14× difference in Tact across the clean/dirty boundary) confirmed by near-replicate samples and consistent with the author-stated correlation. Confidence is bounded below 0.80 by: (1) all 8 samples originate from a single study and single material (Ta), precluding assessment of generalizability; (2) n=8 is small, with only 3 dirty-limit samples; (3) non-monotonicity within the clean-limit subgroup weakens the case for a smooth continuous relationship; (4) unresolved confounding by crystal phase (α- vs. β-Ta) and the derived-field nature of mean_free_path_nm from resistivity data.
writeup.qrem_implications: For the QREM materials-to-device mapping layer, this finding supports including mean free path (or its proxy, residual resistivity ratio) as a predictor of vortex activation temperature in Ta, but the mapping function form is not yet well-constrained. A two-regime model is better supported than a single power-law or linear mapping: a threshold near l ≈ 20–80 nm (the unsampled gap) separates a dirty-limit regime where Tact scales steeply with decreasing l from a clean-limit regime where Tact is low and weakly dependent on l. A practical QREM mapping might take the form Tact = f(l/ξ₀) where ξ₀ is the BCS coherence length, encoding the clean/dirty crossover physically, but this functional form is not yet supportable from 8 samples in one material. Until the intermediate l range is sampled and the phase-purity confound is resolved, any mapping function should be treated as a qualitative regime classifier (clean vs. dirty) rather than a quantitative predictor. Cross-material validation (Nb, NbN, Al) is required before this mapping is incorporated into device-level loss or coherence predictions.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Re-analysis focused on Ta is warranted given the 8 available samples, but only if crystal phase data (α- vs. β-Ta) can be incorporated to deconfound the phase-purity effect. No other material in the current corpus has both mean_free_path_nm and vortex_activation_temperature_K measured, so a per-material re-analysis for Nb, Al, NbN, or NbTiN is not yet possible. Priority should be given to acquiring dirty-to-clean series data for at least one other material (Nb is the most natural choice given its extensive literature) before broadening the QREM mapping.
questions_for_reviewer: Do the Bahrami_2026 samples span both α-Ta and β-Ta phases, and is XRD or electron diffraction data available per sample? If D7/D8 are predominantly β-Ta and D1–D5 are α-Ta, the correlation may be a phase-purity effect rather than a mean-free-path effect, which would substantially change the QREM mapping strategy.

Is the vortex_activation_temperature_K field extracted from an Arrhenius fit to resistivity vs. temperature data in finite magnetic field, or from a different protocol (e.g., flux-flow onset, IV curve inflection)? The physical interpretation and comparability across samples depends critically on the extraction method.

The unsampled gap between l ≈ 20 nm and l ≈ 82 nm is the most uncertain region of the relationship — does the Bahrami_2026 study report any samples in this intermediate range that were excluded from the corpus, or is this gap a genuine feature of the deposition parameter space explored?

Within the clean-limit group, D1 (l=109.3 nm, Tact=0.54 K) and D5 (l=112.2 nm, Tact=0.57 K) have nearly identical mean free paths but are elevated relative to D3 (l=81.5 nm, Tact=0.29 K) and D4 (l=125.2 nm, Tact=0.33 K) — is there any known difference in deposition conditions, film thickness, or substrate between these pairs that could explain the non-monotonicity?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next step is to obtain or generate a systematic series of Ta thin films spanning the intermediate mean free path range (l ≈ 20–80 nm) with concurrent XRD phase identification and Arrhenius-extracted vortex activation temperatures, to determine whether the l–Tact relationship is continuous through the clean/dirty crossover or step-like, and to deconfound the α/β-Ta phase effect. A secondary high-value step is to replicate the measurement in Nb thin films with a comparable dirty-to-clean series, which would test generalizability and allow the QREM mapping to be placed on a multi-material footing.

writeup.title: Increasing DC magnetron sputtering deposition temperature is negatively correlated with superconducting transition temperature in Ta-Hf (83:17) thin films on sapphire.
writeup.hypothesis: For Ta-Hf (83:17) thin films deposited by DC magnetron sputtering on sapphire, increasing deposition temperature is negatively correlated with superconducting transition temperature (Tc).
writeup.summary: Across two independent deposition series (8 samples total, 4 temperature points each: 550–850°C), Tc consistently decreases from ~6.17–6.21 K at 550°C to ~5.84–6.00 K at 850°C, with both series showing the most pronounced suppression between 750°C and 850°C. The total Tc suppression over the 300°C range is modest (~0.21–0.33 K) but reproduced in both series without exception at the high-temperature end. A minor non-monotonicity at the low-temperature end (Tc marginally higher at 650°C than 550°C in series 1) introduces mild uncertainty about strict monotonicity but does not overturn the overall negative trend.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: Both deposition series independently show the lowest Tc values at the highest deposition temperature (850°C): 5.84 K and 6.00 K respectively, versus 6.17 K and 6.21 K at 550°C. The intermediate temperature points (750°C) also show consistent intermediate suppression (6.02 K and 6.06 K), providing a coherent trend across four temperature points in duplicate.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: In both series, Tc at 650°C is marginally equal to or slightly higher than at 550°C (6.20 K vs. 6.17 K in series 1; 6.18 K vs. 6.21 K in series 2), introducing a non-monotonic step at the low-temperature end that is likely within measurement uncertainty but prevents a strictly monotonic interpretation across the full range.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Structural characterization (XRD or TEM) at each deposition temperature is the most critical missing data: without it, it is impossible to determine whether the Tc suppression is driven by phase changes (e.g., α-Ta vs. β-Ta-like phases), grain structure evolution, substrate interdiffusion, or thermal strain — all of which have distinct implications for process optimization.

writeup.finding_detail: The negative correlation is anchored most strongly at the high-temperature end: Yang_2026_Table_2_Row_4 (850°C, series 1, Tc = 5.84 K) and Row_8 (850°C, series 2, Tc = 6.00 K) represent the lowest Tc values in the entire dataset, while Row_1 (550°C, series 1, Tc = 6.17 K) and Row_5 (550°C, series 2, Tc = 6.21 K) represent the highest. The intermediate points (Rows 3 and 7 at 750°C: 6.02 K and 6.06 K) fall consistently between these extremes in both series, supporting a real trend rather than noise. The complicating non-monotonicity at 650°C (Rows 2 and 6: 6.20 K and 6.18 K) is minor — both values remain above the 750°C and 850°C points — and may reflect a slight crystallization optimum near 650°C or simply measurement scatter at the ~0.02–0.04 K level. The effect size is small in absolute terms (~0.2–0.3 K over 300°C), meaning systematic measurement artifacts or uncontrolled process variables (e.g., argon pressure, deposition rate co-varying with temperature) cannot be fully excluded as partial contributors without additional characterization. All 8 samples originate from a single study, single material composition, and single substrate, which limits generalizability but provides strong internal consistency.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most physically plausible alternative is that higher deposition temperature modifies the crystallographic phase, texture, or grain structure of the Ta-Hf alloy film (e.g., promoting phases with lower electron-phonon coupling), which suppresses Tc independently of any direct thermal effect on the superconducting condensate. Substrate interdiffusion or oxygen incorporation at elevated temperatures, and thermal strain from differential expansion between film and sapphire, are secondary alternatives that could also reduce the electronic density of states at the Fermi level.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.72 based on: (1) consistent replication of the negative trend across two independent series at the high-temperature end (750–850°C), with no exceptions; (2) reasonable internal replication with 8 samples across 4 temperature points in duplicate within a single study; (3) mild non-monotonicity at 550–650°C in series 1 that prevents full monotonic confidence and suggests the relationship may not be strictly linear; (4) small absolute effect size (~0.2–0.3 K) that is consistent but within the range where systematic process confounds could contribute; (5) single-study, single-material, single-substrate provenance that limits generalizability and prevents confidence above 0.75; and (6) absence of structural characterization data that would confirm the physical mechanism and rule out confounds.
writeup.qrem_implications: This finding provides weak-to-moderate support for including deposition temperature as a process parameter in a QREM materials-to-device mapping layer for Ta-Hf (83:17) films, but the current data do not yet support a well-defined quantitative mapping function. The trend is consistent with a monotonically decreasing Tc(T_dep) function over 750–850°C, but the non-monotonicity at 550–650°C and the small effect size suggest the relationship may be non-linear or threshold-like rather than smoothly linear across the full range. A provisional mapping of the form Tc ≈ Tc_0 − α·T_dep (with α ~ 0.001 K/°C estimated from the 300°C range) could be used as a weak prior, but should not be treated as a reliable predictive function until structural characterization clarifies the mechanism and data from additional studies or deposition conditions are available. The practical implication for qubit fabrication is that deposition temperatures at or below 650°C appear preferable for maximizing Tc in this alloy system, though the gain over 550°C is marginal.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Re-analysis focused on Ta-Hf (83:17) is warranted given the 8 available samples, but only within the constraints of this single study. No other material classes in the current corpus have comparable deposition-temperature series data for this hypothesis. If additional Ta-Hf data from other studies or compositions (e.g., different Hf fractions) become available, a per-composition re-analysis would be valuable to assess whether the negative trend generalizes across the Ta-Hf alloy family. Pure Ta and TaN films deposited under similar conditions would be the most informative comparators.
questions_for_reviewer: The two deposition series appear to differ slightly in absolute Tc values (series 2 is consistently ~0.04–0.16 K higher than series 1 at matched temperatures). What experimental variable distinguishes the two series — is it deposition duration, film thickness, or another parameter — and could this systematic offset indicate a confound that also affects the temperature trend interpretation?

The non-monotonicity at 650°C (Tc marginally higher than at 550°C in series 1) appears in both series. In your judgment, is this within the expected measurement uncertainty for Tc determination in these films, or does it suggest a physically meaningful optimum near 650°C that should be investigated further?

Are there any known phase boundaries or crystallographic transitions in Ta-Hf (83:17) alloys in the 550–850°C range that would predict a non-linear or threshold Tc response to deposition temperature, rather than the approximately linear suppression suggested by the data?

The hypothesis seed references '1 ks films' — does this refer to deposition time, post-deposition anneal duration, or another process parameter, and does it apply uniformly to all 8 samples or only one series?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next experiment would be XRD characterization of films deposited at each of the four temperatures (550, 650, 750, 850°C) from both series, to determine whether the Tc suppression at high deposition temperatures correlates with a specific phase transition, texture change, or grain size reduction. This would directly test the most plausible alternative explanation (crystallographic phase modification) and, if confirmed, would elevate confidence substantially and provide a mechanistic basis for the QREM mapping function.

writeup.title: Dirty-limit Ta thin films exhibit vortex activation temperatures roughly one order of magnitude higher than clean-limit films from the same study
writeup.hypothesis: In Ta thin films, longer mean free path (cleaner, higher-RRR films) is negatively correlated with vortex activation temperature, such that dirty-limit films exhibit substantially higher vortex activation temperatures than clean-limit films.
writeup.summary: Across 8 Ta thin-film samples from a single study (Bahrami_2026), dirty-limit samples (mean free path l = 12.9–20.4 nm) show vortex activation temperatures of 1.69–4.17 K, while all five clean-limit samples (l = 81.5–142.3 nm) cluster at 0.29–0.57 K — a separation of roughly 3–14× in Tact. The between-regime signal is large and internally replicated, but within the clean-limit cluster the correlation between l and Tact is weak and non-monotonic, indicating that mean free path alone does not continuously predict vortex activation temperature at fine resolution. All data originate from a single material (Ta), single research group, and single deposition method, which substantially limits generalizability.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: The two dirtiest samples (D7: l=12.9 nm, Tact=4.16 K; D8: l=13.3 nm, Tact=4.17 K) are near-identical replicates at the dirty-limit extreme, providing internal validation that the elevated Tact is not a single-sample artifact. The intermediate dirty-limit sample (D6: l=20.4 nm, Tact=1.69 K) sits between the two regimes in a manner consistent with a monotonic trend across the dirty/clean-limit boundary.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Within the clean-limit cluster, the l–Tact relationship is non-monotonic: D3 (l=81.5 nm) has the lowest Tact (0.29 K) despite the shortest clean-limit mean free path, while D5 (l=112.2 nm) has the highest clean-limit Tact (0.57 K) despite not having the longest l. This scatter indicates that additional variables modulate Tact within the clean limit independently of mean free path.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Intermediate mean free path samples (l ~ 30–80 nm) are absent, leaving the transition between regimes unresolved; independent replication from a second deposition system or research group is needed to confirm the trend is not process-specific.

writeup.finding_detail: The dataset separates cleanly into two regimes at approximately l ~ 40–80 nm. Dirty-limit samples D7 (l=12.9 nm, Tact=4.16 K), D8 (l=13.3 nm, Tact=4.17 K), and D6 (l=20.4 nm, Tact=1.69 K) all exceed the highest clean-limit Tact (D5: 0.57 K) by factors of 3–7×, and the two dirtiest samples exceed it by ~7×. The clean-limit samples D1–D5 (l=81.5–142.3 nm) span only 0.29–0.57 K in Tact despite a ~1.7× range in l, and the ordering is not monotonic (e.g., D4 at l=125.2 nm has Tact=0.33 K while D5 at l=112.2 nm has Tact=0.57 K). Both mean free path and vortex activation temperature are model-derived quantities: l is typically computed from resistivity and RRR using bulk scattering parameters that may not apply to thin films, and Tact is extracted from thermally-activated flux-flow fits. The magnitude of the between-regime separation (~one order of magnitude) is large enough that moderate systematic errors in either derived quantity are unlikely to eliminate the signal, but this model-dependency is a non-trivial caveat. The single-study, single-material, single-deposition-method provenance of all 8 samples means the pattern could reflect a specific process parameter (e.g., sputtering pressure) that co-varies with both l and Tact without a direct causal link.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that the true driver is microstructural disorder (defect and grain boundary density) rather than mean free path per se: disorder simultaneously reduces l and increases pinning center density, raising vortex activation energy. In this interpretation, l is a proxy for disorder rather than the direct physical cause of Tact differences, and the regime-level separation reflects a qualitative change in vortex pinning physics (e.g., collective vs. single-vortex creep) rather than a smooth continuous dependence on l.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.70 reflecting a robust regime-level signal (3–14× separation in Tact, internally replicated by near-identical D7/D8 pair) that is consistent with the author-stated correlation. Confidence is capped below 0.80 due to: (1) n=8 from a single study and single material with no cross-group replication; (2) non-monotonic within-cluster behavior in the clean-limit regime indicating l is not a fine-grained continuous predictor; (3) model-dependency of both derived fields (l from resistivity, Tact from flux-flow fits); and (4) absence of intermediate l samples to characterize the transition region.
writeup.qrem_implications: For the QREM materials-to-device mapping layer, this finding supports a regime-conditional rather than a smooth continuous mapping function. A two-branch model is better supported than a single linear or power-law fit: dirty-limit Ta films (l ≲ ~20 nm) map to Tact ~ 1.7–4.2 K, while clean-limit films (l ≳ ~80 nm) map to Tact ~ 0.3–0.6 K. A continuous mapping function of the form Tact = f(l) is not yet supportable given the non-monotonic within-cluster behavior and the gap in intermediate l values. For device-relevant applications where low vortex activation temperature is desired (to minimize flux noise and quasiparticle generation), this finding suggests that achieving clean-limit Ta (l > ~80 nm) is a meaningful materials target, with Tact expected to drop below ~0.6 K. However, the mapping should be flagged as single-source and Ta-specific until cross-material and cross-group data are available.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Ta is the only material in this corpus and has 8 samples — sufficient to warrant a focused re-analysis specifically for Ta, particularly to examine whether the regime-level separation holds across different deposition methods and substrates. No other material has samples in this dataset for this hypothesis pair. Re-analysis for Nb, NbN, and Al would be valuable if samples with both mean free path and vortex activation temperature measurements can be identified, but cannot be recommended on current data.
questions_for_reviewer: Are the mean free path values for these Ta thin films computed using bulk Ta scattering parameters, and if so, how large is the expected systematic error for films in the 10–150 nm thickness range — could this shift the dirty/clean-limit boundary estimate significantly?

Is the vortex activation temperature extracted here from a single thermally-activated flux-flow model (Arrhenius fit to resistivity vs. T), or from a more detailed analysis such as the Zeldov or collective creep model? The choice of model can shift Tact by factors that matter within the clean-limit cluster.

Do the dirty-limit samples (D6, D7, D8) differ from the clean-limit samples in film thickness, substrate, or post-deposition treatment in ways that could independently explain the large Tact difference, beyond the mean free path difference?

Within the clean-limit cluster, D3 (l=81.5 nm) has the lowest Tact (0.29 K) despite the shortest clean-limit l — does the author provide any microstructural characterization (e.g., XRD, TEM) that might explain this inversion, such as differences in grain size or phase purity?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-priority experiment is to fabricate Ta films with intermediate mean free paths (l ~ 30–80 nm) under controlled conditions to map the transition region between dirty- and clean-limit behavior continuously, and to pair these with direct pinning landscape measurements (e.g., magneto-optical imaging or scanning Hall probe) to determine whether the Tact change reflects a change in pinning energy scale, pinning center density, or vortex creep regime. A secondary priority is independent replication by a second group using a different deposition system to confirm the trend is not process-specific.

writeup.title: Superconducting critical temperature in NbSe2 thin films increases monotonically with film thickness up to ~8 nm, saturating near the bulk value of 7.0–7.2 K.
writeup.hypothesis: In NbSe2 thin films, increasing film thickness is positively associated with higher superconducting critical temperature (Tc), reflecting thickness-dependent suppression of superconductivity in the ultra-thin limit.
writeup.summary: Across 6 NbSe2 thin-film samples from a single study (Zaman_2025), Tc rises monotonically from 3.5 K at 1.99 nm to 7.0 K at ~8 nm, after which it saturates near the known bulk NbSe2 value. The trend is physically well-motivated and internally consistent, but all data derive from one research group with no independent replication, and the mechanistic driver—disorder, quantum size effects, or substrate proximity—cannot be distinguished from the available measurements alone. The finding is assessed as partially supported at moderate confidence.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: All 6 samples follow a strictly monotonic Tc-vs-thickness trend from 3.5 K (D1, 1.99 nm) to 7.0 K (D5, 7.98 nm), with correlated transport-regime transitions (dirty → intermediate → near-clean limit) that are physically consistent with disorder-driven Tc suppression recovering as thickness increases.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: D5 (7.98 nm) and D8 (10.07 nm) both show Tc = 7.0 K despite a ~25% thickness difference, indicating saturation rather than a linear relationship throughout; the Tc measurement for D8 also carries a 'medium' confidence tag, introducing minor additional uncertainty.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Independent replication from a second research group, and paired sheet-resistance or RRR measurements for each sample, are the most critical missing data needed to confirm the trend and distinguish disorder-driven from quantum-size-effect-driven Tc suppression.

writeup.finding_detail: The Tc-vs-thickness relationship in this dataset is unambiguously positive and monotonic across the 2–8 nm range: D1 (1.99 nm, Tc = 3.5 K), D2 (4.08 nm, 4.8 K), D3 (5.86 nm, 5.5 K), D4 (7.13 nm, 6.0 K), D5 (7.98 nm, 7.0 K), and D8 (10.07 nm, 7.0 K). The steepest Tc gain occurs below ~4 nm, consistent with the ultra-thin dirty-limit regime where surface and interface scattering dominate pair-breaking. The functional form is best described as asymptotic rather than linear: Tc rises rapidly below ~6 nm and saturates near the bulk NbSe2 value (~7.2 K) by ~8 nm, meaning the hypothesis holds most strongly in the ultra-thin regime. The coincident transport-regime evolution (dirty → intermediate → near-clean) across the same thickness range provides qualitative mechanistic support, but does not uniquely identify whether disorder, quantum confinement, CDW competition, or substrate proximity is the primary driver. The seeding literature citation (Chang_2025, Ta films with Au encapsulation) is mechanistically distinct—proximity-effect pair-breaking by a normal-metal overlayer—and does not directly support or contradict the NbSe2 result.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative to an intrinsic quantum-size effect is thickness-dependent disorder: thinner films enter the dirty limit with higher sheet resistance, and enhanced pair-breaking from surface/interface scattering suppresses Tc without requiring any quantum confinement physics. A secondary alternative is that the discrete van der Waals layer structure of NbSe2 means the apparent thickness dependence actually reflects layer-number-quantized changes in phonon coupling and CDW competition rather than a smooth continuum effect.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.62 reflecting a clear, physically motivated, and internally consistent monotonic trend across all 6 samples, offset by three significant limitations: (1) all data originate from a single study (Zaman_2025) with no independent replication, which is the dominant uncertainty; (2) n=6 is small, limiting statistical robustness; and (3) the saturation of Tc at D5/D8 means the functional form is asymptotic rather than linear, and the mechanistic driver cannot be identified from Tc measurements alone. The complementary prior finding on BCS gap vs. thickness (confidence 0.55) provides mild corroborating support but is mathematically redundant with Tc and does not add independent evidence.
writeup.qrem_implications: This finding provides partial support for a thickness-dependent Tc mapping function in the QREM materials-to-device layer for NbSe2, but the mapping function is not yet fully specifiable. The data support a monotonically increasing, asymptotic functional form—plausibly a power law or exponential saturation of the form Tc(d) = Tc_bulk × [1 − exp(−d/d0)]—in the 2–10 nm range, with saturation near 7.0–7.2 K above ~8 nm. However, a quantitative mapping function should not be committed to the QREM layer until: (a) independent replication confirms the trend, and (b) the mechanistic driver is identified, since disorder-driven and quantum-size-effect-driven suppression would predict different functional forms and different sensitivities to fabrication process variations. For now, the QREM layer could encode a qualitative flag: NbSe2 films below ~6 nm should be expected to have substantially suppressed Tc relative to bulk, with high sensitivity to thickness control.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: This hypothesis should be re-run as a focused per-material analysis for NbSe2 specifically, as it is the only material with sufficient paired Tc/thickness measurements in the current corpus (n=6). No other material in the corpus has enough samples with both fields measured to support a comparable focused re-analysis at this time. If additional NbTiN, MoRe, or TaN thin-film data with thickness measurements are added to the corpus, those materials would become candidates for analogous analyses.
questions_for_reviewer: Is the saturation of Tc at ~7.0 K for both D5 (7.98 nm) and D8 (10.07 nm) consistent with the known bulk NbSe2 Tc, or does it suggest a systematic underestimate in the Zaman_2025 measurements (bulk NbSe2 Tc is typically cited as 7.2 K)?

Given that NbSe2 is a van der Waals material with discrete monolayer thickness (~0.65 nm per layer), should the thickness axis be reinterpreted as layer count (approximately 3, 6, 9, 11, 12, 15 layers for D1–D8), and does the reviewer expect the Tc-vs-layer-number relationship to be smooth or to show discrete jumps at specific layer counts?

The transport regime labels (dirty, intermediate, near-clean) are qualitative—does the reviewer have access to the raw sheet resistance or RRR values from Zaman_2025 that would allow a more quantitative assessment of whether disorder alone accounts for the observed Tc suppression?

Is the Si substrate used in Zaman_2025 high-resistivity or standard-resistivity, and could substrate-induced strain or interface phonon coupling at sub-2 nm thicknesses plausibly contribute to the Tc suppression seen in D1, independent of disorder or quantum size effects?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next step is to identify or solicit an independent dataset of NbSe2 thin films with paired Tc and thickness measurements from a second research group, ideally including samples below 2 nm (monolayer/bilayer) and above 10 nm, with simultaneous sheet resistance measurements. This would simultaneously address the replication gap, confirm or refute the saturation behavior, and enable disorder-effect decoupling—resolving the three largest sources of uncertainty in a single experiment.

writeup.title: No cross-material correlation between film thickness and superconducting critical temperature is supported; a within-material effect is confirmed only for ultra-thin NbSe2.
writeup.hypothesis: Film thickness is a meaningful predictor of superconducting critical temperature (Tc) in superconducting thin films used for quantum computing applications, such that a mapping function Tc = f(thickness) could be constructed across or within materials.
writeup.summary: Across 43 samples spanning 9 material systems, no coherent cross-material relationship between film thickness and Tc is observed; material identity, deposition method, substrate, and post-deposition annealing dominate Tc variation. The only well-supported within-material thickness-Tc relationship is in NbSe2 (6 samples, 2–10 nm), where Tc rises monotonically from 3.5 K to 7.0 K as thickness increases — a known 2D superconductor quantum-confinement effect, not a generic thin-film phenomenon. For all other materials in this corpus (NbN, Ta, Ta-Hf, Al, PtSi, Re), either no thickness variation exists within the material or the apparent thickness signal is confounded by process and substrate differences.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: NbSe2 samples (Zaman_2025, D1–D5, D8) show a clear, monotonic positive Tc–thickness relationship from 3.5 K at ~2 nm to 7.0 K at ~10 nm, with apparent saturation near bulk Tc, consistent with well-established 2D superconductor physics. This is the only material in the corpus with sufficient within-material thickness variation to assess the hypothesis directly.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: All other materials lack within-material thickness variation: all 9 Al samples are at 200 nm, all 6 PtSi samples at 25 nm, and all Re samples at 150 nm, structurally preventing thickness-Tc analysis for the majority of the corpus. NbN shows non-monotonic apparent thickness dependence that is fully explained by differing deposition methods (ALD vs. ALD+ALE thinning) and annealing conditions rather than thickness per se. The single author-stated seeding correlation (Chang_2025) concerns Au encapsulation proximity-effect suppression of the effective gap in a bilayer geometry — a physically distinct mechanism from intrinsic single-layer film Tc vs. self-thickness.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Thickness series within a single material at fixed deposition conditions and substrate — e.g., Ta or NbN films deposited identically across a range of 10–300 nm — are entirely absent from this corpus and are the minimum requirement to test the hypothesis for any material other than NbSe2.

writeup.finding_detail: The NbSe2 result (Zaman_2025 D1–D8) is the only clean signal in the dataset: six samples spanning 2–10 nm show a monotonic Tc increase (3.5 → 7.0 K) consistent with suppression of superconductivity in the 2D ultra-thin limit, a well-understood phenomenon driven by enhanced quantum fluctuations and disorder at reduced dimensionality. This effect saturates near 10 nm, consistent with approach to the bulk NbSe2 Tc (~7.2 K), and is not generalizable to other materials. For NbN, the apparent thickness range (5–50 nm across Böttcher_2025 and Wu_2026) is confounded by different ALD chamber conditions, ALE thinning steps, and a 1000°C anneal in one study, making any thickness-Tc inference unreliable. Ta-Hf (83:17) samples at 250 nm and 470 nm (Yang_2026) show statistically indistinguishable Tc values (mean ~6.06 K vs. ~6.11 K), directly contradicting a meaningful thickness effect in this alloy at these scales. Ta samples across studies (Potluri_2025 at 100–150 nm, Bland_2025 at 200 nm) show a marginal apparent Tc decrease (4.4 → 4.2 K over 100 nm) that is within the range attributable to substrate differences (Si vs. Al2O3) and inter-study variability. The seeding correlation from Chang_2025 describes Cooper pair leakage into a Au encapsulation layer (proximity effect) rather than intrinsic Tc dependence on film self-thickness, and carries no direct evidential weight for this hypothesis; furthermore, any derived BCS gap fields (Δ = 1.764 k_B Tc) are mathematically identical to Tc and provide no independent corroboration.
writeup.alternative_explanations: Material identity is the dominant determinant of Tc across this dataset, with intrinsic Tc spanning 0.9–12 K reflecting bulk material properties rather than thickness effects. Within individual materials, deposition technique, substrate choice, and post-deposition annealing temperature appear to be far stronger predictors of Tc than film thickness in the 20–200 nm regime where most samples reside — well above the ultra-thin limits where thickness-driven Tc suppression is physically expected.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.15 (very low support for the general hypothesis). The NbSe2 within-material result is well-supported by 6 samples with clean monotonic behavior, but it is material-specific and covers only the ultra-thin 2D regime. For all other materials, the structural absence of within-material thickness variation (single-thickness clusters for Al, PtSi, Re) or uncontrolled confounders (NbN, Ta) means the hypothesis cannot be evaluated with the available data. The single author-stated correlation is from a physically distinct bilayer mechanism. The overall corpus of 43 samples provides essentially no leverage on the cross-material hypothesis, and only weak leverage on within-material hypotheses for materials other than NbSe2.
writeup.qrem_implications: A universal or cross-material mapping function Tc = f(thickness) is not supported and should not be implemented in the QREM materials-to-device mapping layer. For NbSe2 specifically, a within-material monotonic mapping function is empirically supported in the 2–10 nm range (approximately Tc ≈ 7.0 × [1 − exp(−d/τ)] K with saturation near 10 nm), but this is a material-specific 2D physics effect and not a transferable functional form. For all other materials in this corpus, thickness should not be used as a predictor of Tc in the mapping layer without material-specific, process-controlled thickness series data. The QREM layer should instead prioritize material identity, deposition method, and substrate as primary Tc predictors, with thickness as a secondary or conditional variable only for materials known to exhibit strong thickness dependence (e.g., 2D superconductors, films below ~10 nm).
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Yes, this hypothesis should be re-run per material class if and when appropriate within-material thickness series data become available. Current sample counts per material with thickness variation: NbSe2 (6 samples, 2–10 nm range — sufficient for a focused within-material analysis, already conducted here); NbN (3 samples across 5–50 nm but with uncontrolled process confounders — insufficient without process metadata); Ta (3 samples across 100–200 nm but across different studies and substrates — insufficient); Ta-Hf (8 samples at only 2 thickness values — marginal, but the null result at 250 vs. 470 nm is informative); Al (9 samples all at 200 nm — no thickness variation, cannot be re-run); PtSi (6 samples all at 25 nm — no thickness variation, cannot be re-run); Re (5 samples all at 150 nm — no thickness variation, cannot be re-run). Priority for re-analysis: NbSe2 (data exists, analysis complete), NbN (high scientific interest, needs process-controlled series), Ta (high quantum computing relevance, needs systematic thickness study).
questions_for_reviewer: The NbSe2 Tc–thickness relationship (Zaman_2025) is treated here as a 2D quantum-confinement effect distinct from generic thin-film behavior. Is this interpretation correct for the specific NbSe2 samples in this corpus (exfoliated vs. CVD vs. sputtered), and does the saturation near 10 nm match the expected crossover thickness for this material?

For NbN, the 5 nm sample (Wu_2026) shows Tc = 10.0 K while the 20 nm ALD+ALE sample (Böttcher_2025) shows Tc = 12.0 K and the 50 nm annealed sample (Wu_2026) shows Tc = 10.0 K. Is the 12 K value for 20 nm NbN physically plausible given the known NbN bulk Tc (~16 K), or does it suggest an anomalously high-quality film where the ALE thinning and sapphire substrate are the dominant factors rather than thickness?

The seeding correlation from Chang_2025 is attributed to Au encapsulation proximity effect on the effective gap, not intrinsic Tc. Can you confirm whether Chang_2025 reports a direct Tc measurement for the Ta film itself (independent of the Au layer), and if so, whether Tc varies with Au thickness or only the effective gap does?

For Ta samples across Potluri_2025 (100–150 nm, Si substrate) and Bland_2025 (200 nm, Al2O3 substrate), the apparent Tc decrease of ~0.2 K over 100 nm is within inter-study variability. Is there any physical reason to expect a genuine thickness-Tc relationship in Ta at these scales (well above the coherence length), or should this be treated as noise?

Given that 111 of 154 corpus samples lack either Tc or thickness measurements, is there a systematic reason why these fields co-occur only in 43 samples — e.g., are thickness measurements preferentially reported for certain study types (materials characterization vs. device performance papers) — and does this selection bias affect the representativeness of the 43-sample subset?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value experiment to resolve this hypothesis would be a controlled thickness series for Ta or NbN — two materials of primary quantum computing relevance — deposited by a single method (e.g., DC magnetron sputtering or ALD) on a fixed substrate (e.g., c-plane sapphire) at thicknesses spanning 5–300 nm, with Tc measured by four-probe resistance vs. temperature for each film. This would isolate thickness as a variable while holding all other parameters constant, providing the within-material thickness-Tc data that is entirely absent from the current corpus for these materials. Simultaneous measurement of sheet resistance and RRR would allow separation of disorder-driven Tc suppression from geometric thickness effects.

An intermediate thickness (150 nm, Ta on Al2O3) shows intermediate Tc (4.3 K), consistent with a monotonic ordering across thickness values.

Yet it shares the same thickness (200 nm) and Tc (4.2 K) as the pure Ta Bland_2025 sample, suggesting the Al/AlOx junction does not alter Tc here, but also that this sample may not cleanly test the hypothesis for pure Ta films.

writeup.title: No meaningful correlation between film thickness and superconducting critical temperature is detectable in 100–200 nm Ta films across four available samples.
writeup.hypothesis: In Ta thin films, film thickness is meaningfully correlated with superconducting critical temperature (Tc), such that thicker films exhibit lower or higher Tc in a systematic and physically significant way.
writeup.summary: Across four Ta samples spanning 100–200 nm thickness, Tc varies only between 4.2 and 4.4 K — a spread of 0.2 K that is likely within typical measurement uncertainty and batch-to-batch deposition variability. Although a monotonic ordering (thinner → higher Tc) is visible in the data, this apparent trend is confounded by substrate differences (Si vs. Al2O3), cross-laboratory deposition conditions, and one composite film (Ta with Al/AlOx junction), making it impossible to attribute the small Tc variation to thickness alone. The absence of a robust thickness–Tc relationship in this regime is itself an informative negative result, consistent with the physical expectation that thickness-driven Tc suppression in Ta is primarily a sub-20 nm phenomenon.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: The three pure Ta samples show a monotonically ordered Tc with thickness — Potluri_2025_Table_I_Row_1 (100 nm, Si substrate, Tc = 4.4 K), Potluri_2025_Table_I_Row_2 (150 nm, Al2O3 substrate, Tc = 4.3 K), and Bland_2025_Ta-on-Si (200 nm, Si substrate, Tc = 4.2 K) — which is at least directionally consistent with a weak negative thickness–Tc relationship.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: The total Tc range is only 0.2 K across a 2× change in thickness, and no two samples share both the same substrate and deposition system, meaning substrate identity and lab-specific deposition conditions are fully confounded with thickness across the dataset; the single author-stated correlation seed (Chang_2025) concerns Au-overlayer proximity suppression of the superconducting gap, a mechanistically distinct phenomenon from intrinsic Ta film thickness effects on Tc.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Matched pairs of Ta films deposited under identical conditions on the same substrate with systematically varied thickness (ideally spanning 20–500 nm), accompanied by stated Tc measurement uncertainties and α-Ta vs. β-Ta phase fraction characterization, are needed to decouple thickness effects from substrate and deposition confounds.

writeup.finding_detail: The four samples cluster tightly in Tc (4.2–4.4 K) relative to the bulk α-Ta Tc of ~4.47 K, suggesting all films are predominantly in the α-Ta phase and that no strong thickness-driven suppression is operative in the 100–200 nm range. The apparent monotonic ordering — Potluri_2025_Table_I_Row_1 (100 nm, Si, 4.4 K) > Potluri_2025_Table_I_Row_2 (150 nm, Al2O3, 4.3 K) > Bland_2025_Ta-on-Si and Bland_2025_45-qubit_ensemble (200 nm, 4.2 K) — cannot be attributed to thickness because the 100 nm and 150 nm samples differ in both substrate (Si vs. Al2O3) and laboratory (Potluri_2025 DC sputtering vs. Bland_2025 UHV AJA Orion 8). The inclusion of Bland_2025_45-qubit_ensemble, a composite Ta/Al/AlOx film, adds a further material-system confound, though its Tc matches the pure Ta Bland_2025 sample at the same thickness, suggesting the Al/AlOx junction does not alter Tc in this case. The 0.2 K Tc spread is plausibly explained by deposition-condition variability alone, without invoking any thickness dependence. Critically, the thickness regime studied (100–200 nm) is well above the sub-20 nm range where quantum size effects and surface/interface scattering are known to suppress Tc in Ta, so the absence of a strong effect is physically expected.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative explanation is that the small observed Tc variation (0.2 K) reflects lab-to-lab deposition variability and possible differences in α-Ta vs. β-Ta phase fraction — both of which are known to influence Ta Tc more strongly than film thickness in the 100–200 nm regime — rather than any intrinsic thickness dependence. Substrate identity (Si vs. Al2O3) may also contribute independently, as epitaxial strain and interface scattering differ between these substrates.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.15 (very low) because: (1) only 4 samples are available, providing minimal statistical power; (2) the Tc range of 0.2 K is likely within or near typical measurement uncertainty for Ta films and is not accompanied by stated error bars in the source records; (3) thickness, substrate, and deposition system are fully confounded across samples, preventing isolation of any thickness effect; (4) the single author-stated seed correlation (Chang_2025) is mechanistically inapplicable to this dataset; and (5) the physics of Ta films does not predict meaningful Tc variation in the 100–200 nm range. The confidence is not zero because the monotonic ordering is present and directionally consistent, but it is not robust.
writeup.qrem_implications: A thickness-to-Tc mapping function for Ta in the 100–200 nm range is not supportable from this dataset. Even if the weak monotonic trend were real, a 0.2 K variation in Tc would translate to a ~0.03 meV variation in the BCS gap (ΔE ≈ 1.764 k_B ΔTc), which is unlikely to be a dominant driver of device-level qubit performance differences. For QREM purposes, Ta Tc should currently be treated as a near-constant material parameter (~4.2–4.4 K) in the device-relevant thickness range, with deposition phase (α vs. β Ta) and substrate identity flagged as higher-priority variables for Tc prediction. A mapping function of the form Tc(d) for Ta would require data spanning at least 20–500 nm with controlled confounds before it could be parameterized.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: With only 4 Ta samples (including one composite Ta/Al/AlOx system), there are insufficient data for a statistically meaningful per-material re-analysis of Ta alone. No other material class in this corpus has paired Tc and thickness measurements. A focused re-analysis of Ta is warranted only after ingesting additional records — specifically, studies that systematically vary Ta film thickness under controlled deposition conditions. The Ta/Al/AlOx composite sample (Bland_2025_45-qubit_ensemble) should be tracked as a separate material subclass in future analyses to avoid conflating it with pure Ta.
questions_for_reviewer: Are the Tc measurement uncertainties (or transition widths ΔTc) available for any of these four samples? If the measurement precision is ±0.1 K or worse, the entire 0.2 K spread in this dataset is within noise, and the finding should be reclassified as 'no detectable effect' rather than 'weak negative trend.'

Is there any phase characterization (XRD or RBS confirming α-Ta vs. β-Ta fraction) reported for these samples? If the Bland_2025 200 nm films have a higher β-Ta fraction than the Potluri_2025 100 nm film, phase content — not thickness — would be the correct explanatory variable, and the hypothesis should be reframed accordingly.

The seed correlation from Chang_2025 concerns Au encapsulation thickness and proximity-effect gap suppression. Was this seed intended to motivate a proximity-effect hypothesis (Au overlayer on Ta) rather than an intrinsic Ta thickness hypothesis? If so, the hypothesis as currently framed may be misaligned with the seed's intended scope, and a separate hypothesis for Au-overlayer thickness vs. effective gap should be evaluated.

Do any of the Potluri_2025 or Bland_2025 papers report Tc for Ta films at thicknesses outside the 100–200 nm range (e.g., in supplementary data or earlier publications from the same groups)? Such data, even if not in the current corpus, could inform whether a thickness dependence exists at more extreme values.
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next step is to identify or commission a dataset of Ta films deposited in a single system on a single substrate type, spanning 20–500 nm thickness, with Tc measured by four-probe resistance and reported with transition width ΔTc, alongside XRD phase characterization. This would decouple thickness from substrate and deposition confounds and would span the physically motivated regime (sub-50 nm) where thickness-driven Tc suppression is expected. Alternatively, a systematic literature search for Ta thin film Tc vs. thickness studies (e.g., Barquist et al., Chubov et al., or more recent quantum computing foundry reports) could rapidly expand the corpus without new experiments.

A 250 nm film with Tc 6.17 K is close to the 470 nm mean, providing no clear thickness-dependent signal but contributing to the thin evidence base.

writeup.title: No meaningful correlation between film thickness and superconducting critical temperature is observed in Ta-Hf (83:17) thin films across the 250–470 nm range.
writeup.hypothesis: In Ta-Hf (83:17) thin films, film thickness is meaningfully correlated with superconducting critical temperature (Tc), such that thicker films exhibit a systematically different Tc.
writeup.summary: Across 8 Ta-Hf (83:17) samples from a single study (Yang_2026) spanning only two thickness values (250 nm and 470 nm), the mean Tc difference between groups is approximately 0.05 K — smaller than the within-group spread of up to 0.36 K — providing no statistical or directional basis for a thickness-Tc correlation. The highest individual Tc (6.21 K) belongs to a 470 nm film, but the second highest (6.20 K) is a 250 nm film, directly undermining any monotonic trend. This is a well-characterized null result: in the 250–470 nm range, Ta-Hf (83:17) Tc appears insensitive to thickness variation.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: The 470 nm group has a marginally higher mean Tc (~6.11 K) than the 250 nm group (~6.06 K), and the highest individual Tc in the dataset (6.21 K) belongs to a 470 nm sample, which could weakly suggest a positive thickness-Tc relationship. However, this difference is not statistically meaningful given the within-group variance.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: A 250 nm film (Row 2) achieves Tc = 6.20 K, nearly identical to the highest 470 nm value (6.21 K), while the lowest Tc in the dataset (5.84 K, Row 4) is also from a 250 nm film — demonstrating that within-group variability at fixed thickness exceeds any between-group signal. The 470 nm group's lowest Tc (6.00 K) fully overlaps with the 250 nm range, ruling out a thickness-driven floor or ceiling effect.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Tc measurements at additional thickness values — particularly in the ultra-thin regime (10–100 nm) and above 500 nm — from at least one independent deposition run, with all other parameters held fixed, are essential to determine whether a thickness-dependent Tc regime exists outside the narrow 250–470 nm window studied here.

writeup.finding_detail: The 8 samples divide into two groups of 4 replicates each at 250 nm and 470 nm, all from Yang_2026 and deposited under nominally identical conditions. The 250 nm group yields Tc values of 6.17, 6.20, 6.02, and 5.84 K (mean 6.06 K, range 0.36 K); the 470 nm group yields 6.21, 6.18, 6.06, and 6.00 K (mean 6.11 K, range 0.21 K). The between-group mean difference of ~0.05 K is well within the within-group spread and is not statistically distinguishable from zero with n=4 per group. The seeding literature claim (Chang_2025) involves proximity-effect suppression of the superconducting gap in Ta+Au bilayers — a physically distinct mechanism (inter-material proximity coupling) that does not directly predict an intrinsic thickness-Tc relationship in a single-phase Ta-Hf alloy film. Furthermore, both thickness values likely place all samples in a bulk-like transport regime where coherence length is short relative to film thickness, making thickness-dependent Tc suppression physically implausible at these scales. The null result is therefore both statistically and physically consistent.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible interpretation is that both 250 nm and 470 nm exceed the thickness threshold below which disorder or surface/interface effects would suppress Tc in Ta-Hf alloy films, placing all samples in a bulk-like regime where Tc is governed by alloy composition and deposition stoichiometry rather than thickness. Within-group Tc variance (~0.36 K at 250 nm) most likely reflects minor batch-to-batch fluctuations in deposition conditions or stoichiometry rather than any thickness-driven effect.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.1 (no meaningful pattern supported) rather than 0.0 because the null result is itself informative and physically interpretable, but the data are severely limited: only two distinct thickness values, all from a single source (Yang_2026), with no independent replication across studies or deposition runs. The between-group difference (~0.05 K) is smaller than within-group variance, providing no statistical basis for a correlation. The seeding literature analogy (Chang_2025) involves a different physical mechanism and material system, further weakening the prior for this hypothesis. A confidence of 0.1 reflects that the hypothesis is unsupported by available data, not merely untested.
writeup.qrem_implications: A thickness-to-Tc mapping function for Ta-Hf (83:17) is not supportable from current data. In the QREM materials-to-device mapping layer, thickness should not be used as a predictor of Tc for this alloy in the 250–470 nm range; doing so would introduce spurious variance. If a mapping function were eventually warranted (contingent on data spanning a wider thickness range, including the ultra-thin regime), it would likely take the form of a saturation curve — Tc constant above some critical thickness threshold, with suppression only below ~50–100 nm — rather than a linear or monotonic relationship across technologically relevant thicknesses. Until such data exist, thickness should be treated as a non-informative covariate for Ta-Hf Tc prediction. Additionally, reviewers should note that any prior QREM finding reporting a gap-thickness correlation for Ta (derived via BCS from Tc) should be flagged: if Tc shows no thickness dependence in the Ta-family at these scales, the derived gap-thickness trend is likely an artifact of within-dataset Tc scatter propagated through the BCS formula, not a genuine physical relationship.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: A focused re-analysis of thickness-Tc relationships is not warranted for Ta-Hf (83:17) until data spanning a broader thickness range (particularly <100 nm) are available. Among Ta-family materials more broadly, pure Ta films — if represented across a wider thickness range in the corpus — would be the highest-priority candidate for re-analysis, given that thickness-dependent Tc suppression is physically expected in the ultra-thin limit for BCC/β-Ta films. No other single material in the current 8-sample corpus has sufficient thickness diversity to support a meaningful focused re-analysis.
questions_for_reviewer: Is there physical reason to expect a Tc-thickness dependence in Ta-Hf (83:17) alloy films specifically at thicknesses of 250–470 nm, given the dirty-limit transport regime and short coherence length typical of high-Hf-content Ta alloys? Or is bulk-like behavior expected to set in well below 250 nm for this composition?

The seeding correlation (n_stated_by_authors = 1) appears to derive from Chang_2025, which describes proximity-effect suppression in Ta+Au bilayers rather than intrinsic thickness dependence in a single-phase alloy. Can the reviewer confirm whether any primary literature directly reports a thickness-Tc trend for Ta-Hf alloy films, or whether this seed was misattributed?

Given that all 8 samples originate from Yang_2026, is the within-group Tc variance (~0.36 K at 250 nm) consistent with expected run-to-run deposition variability for this system, or does it suggest an uncontrolled experimental variable (e.g., target aging, substrate batch) that should be flagged as a data quality concern?

Should the prior approved finding of a negative gap-thickness correlation in Ta (derived via BCS from Tc) be formally flagged as a potential derived-field artifact in light of this null Tc-thickness result for Ta-Hf? If so, what is the appropriate action — retraction, annotation, or conditional retention pending new data?
writeup.suggested_followup: The most valuable next experiment would be a controlled deposition series for Ta-Hf (83:17) spanning 20–600 nm in at least 5–6 thickness steps, with all other deposition parameters (power, pressure, substrate temperature, target composition) held fixed and a minimum of 3 replicates per thickness, measured by both transport (Tc) and tunneling spectroscopy (gap). This would directly test whether a thickness-dependent Tc suppression regime exists below 250 nm and whether the gap and Tc track each other as expected from BCS theory — resolving both the present null result and the derived-field artifact concern flagged for the prior Ta gap-thickness finding.
