writeup.title: No thickness-Tc correlation can be assessed in Re thin films: all corpus samples share identical thickness (150 nm) and Tc (1.9 K), yielding zero statistical variance.
writeup.hypothesis: In Re thin films used for superconducting qubits, there is a meaningful correlation between film thickness and superconducting critical temperature (Tc_K), analogous to proximity-effect-driven Tc suppression observed in Ta+Au encapsulation systems.
writeup.summary: All 6 Re corpus samples from Wang_2026 share identical values of Tc_K = 1.9 K and film_thickness_nm = 150.0 nm, providing zero variance in either variable and making any correlation analysis statistically impossible. This is a null-variance negative result: the Wang_2026 study treated film thickness as a fixed process parameter rather than an experimental variable, so the corpus is structurally incapable of testing this hypothesis. The seeding literature claim (Chang_2025) describes Au-encapsulation proximity suppression of Ta — a mechanistically distinct scenario that should not be directly extrapolated to intrinsic Tc-thickness dependence in bare Re films.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: There is no supporting evidence for the hypothesis. No sample in the corpus deviates from Tc_K = 1.9 K or film_thickness_nm = 150.0 nm, so no thickness-dependent Tc trend can be identified.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: The complete absence of thickness variation across all 6 samples — including both the unannealed reference film and transmon devices that underwent 1200°C annealing and incorporate Al/AlOx junctions — indicates that Tc in Re at 150 nm is robust to at least these process variations, but no inference about thickness dependence is possible. Additionally, the seeding analogy (Chang_2025, Ta+Au proximity effect) is mechanistically inapplicable to a single-material Re film.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Re films measured across a range of thicknesses (e.g., 5–300 nm) with Tc_K recorded for each, holding deposition conditions, substrate, and anneal constant — analogous to the NbSe2 thickness series in prior approved findings.

writeup.finding_detail: All 6 corpus samples — Wang_2026_Re_film_characterization_sample_(4mm_x_3mm) and Wang_2026_Transmon_1 through Wang_2026_Transmon_5 — report Tc_K = 1.9 K and film_thickness_nm = 150.0 nm without exception. This uniformity reflects experimental design: the Wang_2026 study used a fixed 150 nm Re deposition recipe across all devices, so thickness was never varied as an independent parameter. Notably, even the transmon devices that underwent a 1200°C anneal and incorporate Al/AlOx Josephson junctions show the same Tc as the bare reference film, suggesting Re Tc at this thickness is insensitive to at least these fabrication steps — though this observation cannot be disentangled from thickness effects given the absence of thickness variation. The 150 nm thickness is likely well above the ultra-thin regime (typically <50 nm) where disorder, surface scattering, or proximity effects are expected to suppress Tc in metallic superconductors; Re at 150 nm is plausibly in a bulk-like Tc regime. The seeding claim from Chang_2025 involves a normal-metal (Au) encapsulation suppressing the gap of an adjacent Ta superconductor via proximity effect — a bilayer geometry and mechanism that is not directly analogous to intrinsic thickness dependence of a single-material Re film.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative explanation is that the Wang_2026 corpus was designed to characterize qubit performance at a fixed, optimized Re film thickness rather than to probe thickness-Tc relationships, making the null variance an artifact of experimental scope rather than evidence of thickness-independence. It is also physically plausible that Re Tc is genuinely thickness-independent at 150 nm and above, with any suppression confined to the ultra-thin (<50 nm) regime not sampled here.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at the floor of the scale (0.1) because the dataset provides zero statistical power to evaluate the hypothesis: with no variance in either Tc_K or film_thickness_nm across all 6 samples, no correlation coefficient, trend, or effect size can be computed. The seeding literature analogy is mechanistically inapplicable. No prior approved findings address Re Tc vs. thickness. The 0.1 reflects the irreducible minimum — the hypothesis is not falsified, merely untestable with current data.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM mapping function of the form Tc_K = f(film_thickness_nm) for Re is not supportable from this corpus. The current data permit only a point estimate: Re at 150 nm has Tc_K ≈ 1.9 K, consistent with bulk Re (Tc_bulk ≈ 1.69–2.0 K depending on film quality). No functional form — linear, power-law, or threshold — can be parameterized from a single-point dataset. Until Re films spanning a range of thicknesses are characterized, the QREM mapping layer should treat Re Tc as a fixed material constant (1.9 K) rather than a thickness-dependent variable, and should flag any thickness-Tc mapping for Re as unsupported pending new data.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Re is the only material class represented in this corpus, and with only a single unique (thickness, Tc) data point, a focused per-material re-analysis of Re would not yield additional insight without new data. No other material class in this corpus has sufficient thickness-varied samples to warrant a comparative re-analysis for this hypothesis. Re should be flagged for re-analysis only after a dedicated thickness-series experiment is conducted.
questions_for_reviewer: Is there any published literature reporting Re thin-film Tc as a function of thickness below 100 nm that could be used to seed or contextualize this hypothesis, independent of the Wang_2026 corpus?

Does the 1200°C anneal applied to the Wang_2026 transmon devices affect Re film microstructure (e.g., grain size, crystallographic texture) in a way that might decouple Tc from thickness — and if so, should microstructural parameters be included as covariates in any future thickness-Tc analysis?

Given that Re Tc_bulk is ~1.69 K for bulk crystals but ~1.9 K is commonly reported for Re thin films, is the elevated Tc in Wang_2026 consistent with known strain or disorder effects at 150 nm, and does this suggest the films are already in a non-bulk regime where thickness effects might be expected?

Should the seeding correlation from Chang_2025 (Ta+Au proximity suppression) be formally retired as a seed for Re single-film hypotheses, or is there a reformulation — e.g., Re/substrate proximity effects — that would make it applicable?
writeup.suggested_followup: The most valuable next step is a dedicated Re thin-film deposition series spanning 10–300 nm thickness, with Tc_K measured for each film under identical deposition conditions, substrate preparation, and anneal protocol. This would directly test whether Re exhibits thickness-dependent Tc suppression in the ultra-thin regime and would provide the variance needed to parameterize or rule out a QREM mapping function. Pairing each film with sheet resistance (RRR) measurements would additionally allow disorder effects to be separated from geometric thickness effects.

writeup.title: Weak, heavily confounded negative trend between deposition temperature and Tc observed across three Ta thin film samples from at least two different labs
writeup.hypothesis: For Ta thin films deposited by DC magnetron sputtering, higher deposition temperature is negatively associated with superconducting critical temperature (Tc).
writeup.summary: Across three available Ta samples, Tc decreases monotonically from 4.4 K (room temperature deposition) to 4.2 K (625°C deposition), which is directionally consistent with the hypothesis. However, the total Tc variation is only 0.2 K across a 603°C deposition temperature range — a spread likely within combined measurement uncertainty across labs — and the samples differ in substrate material and deposition system, making it impossible to attribute the trend to deposition temperature alone. The finding is assessed as inconclusive: suggestive but not statistically or scientifically credible with current data.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: All three data points are monotonically ordered in the direction predicted by the hypothesis: Potluri_2025_Table_I_Row_1 (22°C, Tc = 4.4 K), Potluri_2025_Table_I_Row_2 (500°C, Tc = 4.3 K), and Bland_2025_Ta-on-Si (625°C, Tc = 4.2 K), providing at least directional consistency across the full available dataset.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Substrate type varies across samples (Si vs. Al2O3/sapphire) and samples originate from at least two different labs with different deposition systems, introducing uncontrolled confounds that independently affect Ta phase, microstructure, and Tc; the 0.2 K total Tc range is extremely narrow and may not exceed inter-lab systematic offsets or typical transition-width measurement uncertainty.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: A controlled within-lab experiment varying only deposition temperature for Ta on a fixed substrate type, with XRD phase identification (alpha vs. beta Ta), film thickness, and RRR reported for each sample, providing at least 5 data points across a wide temperature range.

writeup.finding_detail: The three available Ta samples span a 603°C deposition temperature range and show a monotonically decreasing Tc: Potluri_2025_Table_I_Row_1 (Si substrate, 22°C) at 4.4 K, Potluri_2025_Table_I_Row_2 (Al2O3 substrate, 500°C) at 4.3 K, and Bland_2025_Ta-on-Si (Si substrate, 625°C, different lab and UHV system) at 4.2 K. The directional consistency is noted, but the 0.2 K total variation is extremely small relative to the temperature range and is likely within the combined systematic uncertainty of Tc measurements across different labs and measurement setups. Critically, the substrate changes between the two Potluri samples (Si to Al2O3), which is known to influence Ta crystalline phase (alpha vs. beta), epitaxial strain, and interfacial scattering — all of which independently affect Tc — making deposition temperature impossible to isolate as the causal variable. Additionally, differences in sputtering system, base pressure, Ar pressure, and power between the Bland and Potluri labs introduce further confounds including potential impurity incorporation and film stoichiometry variation. Without XRD phase identification or film thickness data for all three samples, even the microstructural mechanism underlying the small observed Tc differences cannot be determined.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that the 0.2 K Tc variation reflects substrate-dependent Ta phase stabilization (alpha vs. beta Ta) and/or inter-lab systematic offsets in Tc measurement rather than a genuine deposition-temperature effect; substrate type (Si vs. Al2O3) and deposition system differences are at least as likely as deposition temperature to explain the observed spread.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.35 — above the prior inconclusive baseline of 0.2 for this hypothesis but well below the threshold for a supported finding. Three data points are insufficient for statistical inference, and the monotonic ordering that provides the directional signal is undermined by: (1) a total Tc range of only 0.2 K, likely within combined inter-lab measurement uncertainty; (2) uncontrolled substrate variation (Si vs. Al2O3) between samples; (3) cross-lab comparison introducing deposition system confounds; and (4) absence of phase identification, thickness, or RRR data to constrain alternative explanations. The single author-stated correlation provides weak prior support but does not resolve the confounds identified here.
writeup.qrem_implications: A reliable deposition-temperature-to-Tc mapping function for Ta is not yet supportable from this data. If the hypothesis were confirmed with controlled data, such a mapping function would likely take the form of a weak monotonically decreasing function Tc(T_dep) over the range 22–625°C, with a total Tc modulation of order 0.1–0.3 K — a range that is small but potentially relevant for qubit frequency targeting in quantum devices where Tc precision matters at the ~10 mK level. However, until the confounds (substrate, lab, phase) are resolved, any such mapping function would carry unacceptably large uncertainty for practical QREM use. The QREM layer should flag deposition temperature as a candidate process variable for Ta but should not encode a quantitative mapping until controlled within-lab data are available. Substrate type should be treated as a co-variate in any future mapping model for Ta.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: This hypothesis should be re-run specifically for Ta once a larger, better-controlled dataset is available. Currently, only 3 Ta samples with both deposition temperature and Tc reported exist in the corpus, which is insufficient for a focused per-material re-analysis. No other materials in the current corpus have sufficient samples with both fields measured to warrant a parallel analysis. A targeted data collection effort for Ta — ideally from a single lab varying only deposition temperature on a fixed substrate — would be the prerequisite for a meaningful re-analysis.
questions_for_reviewer: Do the Potluri_2025 and Bland_2025 samples have XRD data confirming whether all three films are alpha-Ta, or is there any possibility that the Tc differences reflect alpha/beta phase mixing rather than a deposition temperature effect per se?

Is 0.2 K Tc variation across these three samples considered physically meaningful for Ta in the context of qubit fabrication, or does this fall within the typical run-to-run variability expected even for nominally identical deposition conditions within a single lab?

The Potluri_2025 samples use different substrates (Si at 22°C vs. Al2O3 at 500°C) — is there a physical reason to expect that Al2O3 substrates at high deposition temperature would suppress Tc relative to Si at room temperature, independent of deposition temperature, that could fully account for the 0.1 K difference between these two samples?

Are there additional Ta thin film datasets in the literature (not yet in the corpus) with controlled deposition temperature series on a fixed substrate that could be ingested to strengthen or refute this finding?
writeup.suggested_followup: The most valuable next step is a controlled within-lab deposition temperature series for Ta on a single fixed substrate (e.g., high-resistivity Si), varying deposition temperature from room temperature to at least 600°C in steps of ~100°C, with XRD phase identification, film thickness, sheet resistance, and four-probe Tc measurement reported for each sample. This would provide the minimum dataset needed to establish or refute a causal deposition-temperature-to-Tc relationship for Ta and to decouple it from substrate and phase effects.

writeup.title: Deposition temperature shows a weak, material-specific negative association with Tc in Ta-Hf alloy films but no consistent cross-material trend across six superconducting thin-film systems.
writeup.hypothesis: Higher deposition temperature is meaningfully associated with lower superconducting critical temperature (Tc) across superconducting thin films used in quantum computing applications.
writeup.summary: Across 28 samples spanning NbN, Re, Ta, Ta-Hf (83:17), and Al, no consistent cross-material relationship between deposition temperature and Tc is observed; material identity dominates Tc by ~10 K, far exceeding any processing-temperature effect. The only controlled within-material series (Ta-Hf 83:17, Yang_2026, n=8 across four temperatures from 550–850°C) shows a weak negative trend of ~0.37 K over 300°C, but this trend is non-monotonic at intermediate temperatures and physically modest. The general cross-material hypothesis is not supported; the Ta-Hf-specific observation warrants cautious follow-up but cannot be generalized.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: Within the Ta-Hf (83:17) series (Yang_2026, samples 21–28), Tc is highest at 550°C (6.17–6.21 K) and lowest at 850°C (5.84–6.0 K), consistent with a weak negative deposition temperature–Tc relationship within this single alloy system on sapphire substrates.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Cross-material comparison is entirely dominated by intrinsic material properties: NbN deposited at 400°C yields Tc=12 K while Re deposited at 900°C yields Tc=1.9 K, making any cross-material regression on deposition temperature physically meaningless. Within Al (Hedrick_2026), apparent Tc differences between deposition temperatures are confounded by substrate changes (c-Al2O3 vs. Si) and transport regime (clean vs. dirty limit), preventing attribution to deposition temperature alone.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Systematic within-material deposition temperature series for NbN, Ta, and Re on fixed substrates with identical deposition methods and controlled film thickness are entirely absent; without these, the generality of even the Ta-Hf trend cannot be assessed.

writeup.finding_detail: The 28-sample corpus spans six material families deposited by at least three distinct methods (ALD, e-beam evaporation, DC sputtering), making cross-material regression on deposition temperature statistically and physically unjustified. Within Ta-Hf (83:17) on sapphire (Yang_2026), Tc declines from 6.21 K at 550°C to 5.84 K at 850°C, but the 650°C replicates (Tc=6.18–6.20 K) slightly exceed some 550°C values, indicating the trend is non-monotonic and the ~0.37 K total variation is comparable to sample-to-sample scatter. For Ta (Potluri_2025 and Bland_2025), Tc spans only 4.2–4.4 K across deposition temperatures of 22°C to 625°C on different substrates (Si and Al2O3), with no discernible trend and substrate identity as an uncontrolled confound. Re films (Wang_2026) are all deposited at 900°C (Tc=1.9 K), providing zero within-material variation, and post-deposition annealing at 1200°C for Transmon samples likely dominates microstructure over deposition temperature. NbN (Bøttcher_2025) contributes only a single deposition temperature (400°C, Tc=12.0 K via ALD), offering no within-material leverage. The Al dataset (Hedrick_2026) conflates deposition temperature with substrate identity and transport regime, rendering it uninterpretable for this hypothesis without additional controls.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that deposition temperature acts indirectly on Tc through microstructural intermediaries—crystalline phase selection, grain size, residual stress, or alloy homogeneity—rather than through a direct thermodynamic effect on the superconducting condensate; this would explain why the effect is material-specific, modest in magnitude, and non-monotonic. Substrate identity, film thickness, deposition rate, and post-deposition annealing are all plausible dominant confounds that covary with deposition temperature across this corpus.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is low (0.28) because only one material system (Ta-Hf, n=8) provides a controlled within-material test, and even there the effect size (~0.37 K over 300°C) is small relative to scatter and non-monotonic at intermediate temperatures. The remaining five material systems are each confounded by substrate differences, deposition method differences, or single-temperature sampling, providing no independent support for the general hypothesis. The 28-sample total is spread too thinly across too many materials and conditions to support a cross-material claim. A prior focused finding on Ta-Hf specifically was assessed at confidence 0.72, which is appropriate for that narrow claim; the generalized cross-material hypothesis assessed here does not inherit that confidence.
writeup.qrem_implications: A universal QREM mapping function of the form Tc = f(deposition_temperature) is not supportable from this corpus. Material identity accounts for ~10 K of Tc variance while deposition temperature accounts for at most ~0.4 K within the one controlled series, meaning any device-layer model that treats deposition temperature as a primary Tc predictor across materials will be dominated by noise and material misidentification errors. If a material-specific mapping is desired for Ta-Hf (83:17) on sapphire, a weak monotonically decreasing function over 550–850°C could be parameterized from Yang_2026 data, but its physical basis (phase vs. grain vs. stress effects) is unresolved, limiting extrapolation confidence. For all other materials in this corpus, deposition temperature should not be encoded as a Tc predictor in the QREM mapping layer until controlled within-material series are available; material identity and substrate should be treated as the primary categorical predictors of Tc at this stage.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Yes, this hypothesis should be re-run per material class rather than cross-material. Ta-Hf (83:17) is the only material with sufficient within-material variation (n=8, 4 temperature points, 2 replicates each) to warrant a focused re-analysis and is recommended for immediate follow-up. Ta has three samples across a wide temperature range (22–625°C) but on different substrates, making it a candidate for re-analysis only if substrate is treated as a covariate or if additional same-substrate samples can be identified. Al, Re, and NbN each have either single deposition temperatures or substrate confounds that make per-material re-analysis premature without new data. A focused Ta-Hf re-analysis should also incorporate structural characterization data (XRD phase, grain size) if available in Yang_2026 supplementary materials.
questions_for_reviewer: For the Ta-Hf (83:17) Yang_2026 series: does the non-monotonic behavior at 650°C (Tc slightly exceeding 550°C in one replicate) reflect a known phase boundary or crystallographic transition in this alloy system at intermediate deposition temperatures, or is it more likely measurement scatter? This would determine whether the weak negative trend is physically meaningful or artifactual.

For the Re films (Wang_2026): the post-deposition anneal at 1200°C is substantially higher than the 900°C deposition temperature — does domain knowledge suggest that anneal temperature dominates over deposition temperature in setting Re film microstructure and Tc, and if so, should anneal temperature be treated as the primary processing variable for Re in the QREM model?

For the Al films (Hedrick_2026): the Tc difference between Al on Si (1.41 K) and Al on c-Al2O3 (1.05–1.15 K) is attributed here to substrate and transport regime effects rather than deposition temperature. Is this interpretation consistent with the broader thin-film Al literature, and is there a known quantitative substrate contribution to Tc suppression in Al on sapphire that could be used to deconfound the deposition temperature effect?

Is there any physical mechanism in the sputtered Ta-Hf system by which a 300°C increase in deposition temperature would be expected to suppress Tc by ~0.37 K — for example, through preferential phase formation (alpha vs. beta Ta-Hf), increased Hf segregation, or grain boundary density changes — and does this magnitude match theoretical expectations for those mechanisms?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value experiment would be a controlled deposition temperature series for Ta on a single substrate (Si or c-Al2O3) spanning 25–700°C with fixed film thickness (~100 nm), deposition rate, and no post-deposition anneal, with concurrent XRD phase identification and Tc measurement. This would test whether the weak negative trend seen in Ta-Hf generalizes to elemental Ta — a more widely used qubit material — and would allow deposition temperature effects to be separated from phase selection effects. As a complementary analysis step, re-examining the Yang_2026 paper for any reported structural characterization (XRD, RBS, or TEM) of the Ta-Hf films across deposition temperatures would determine whether the Tc variation correlates with a structural parameter, which would substantially clarify the physical mechanism and improve QREM model specification.

writeup.title: Deposition temperature shows a non-monotonic, substrate-confounded association with Tc in Al thin films, preventing a reliable predictive relationship from being established.
writeup.hypothesis: In Al thin films deposited by e-beam evaporation, higher deposition temperature is associated with lower superconducting critical temperature (Tc).
writeup.summary: Across 9 Al thin-film samples, a superficially consistent ordering exists within the c-Al2O3 substrate group (Tc ≈ 1.05 K at 200°C vs. 1.15 K at -72°C), but the two highest-Tc samples (1.41 K) are deposited at 25°C on Si substrates, breaking any simple monotonic negative relationship between deposition temperature and Tc. Substrate identity and transport regime (clean vs. dirty limit) appear to be stronger determinants of Tc than deposition temperature alone. The dataset is too sparse, too narrowly clustered, and too confounded by substrate variation to support a quantitative mapping function at this time.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: Six samples deposited at 200°C on c-Al2O3 (Hedrick_2026 Rows 1–2, 4–7) consistently show Tc = 1.05 K, and one cryogenic deposition at -72°C on c-Al2O3 (Row 3) shows Tc = 1.15 K, providing weak within-substrate directional support for the hypothesis.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Two room-temperature (25°C) depositions on Si (Rows 8–9) yield Tc = 1.41 K — the highest values in the dataset — which is higher than the cryogenic c-Al2O3 sample (1.15 K), directly contradicting a monotonic negative deposition-temperature–Tc relationship and implicating substrate as the dominant variable.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Al films deposited on a single substrate type across a systematic temperature range (e.g., -72, 0, 25, 100, 200°C) with all other variables held constant, accompanied by structural characterization (XRD, TEM) and residual resistivity ratio measurements to decouple substrate, disorder, and thermal effects on Tc.

writeup.finding_detail: Within the c-Al2O3 subset (Rows 1–2, 4–7 at 200°C; Row 3 at -72°C), Tc spans only 1.05–1.15 K, and the cryogenic sample (Row 3) is identified as dirty-limit while the 200°C samples are clean-limit, suggesting that the small Tc difference may reflect transport regime rather than a direct thermal deposition effect. The six 200°C samples provide no within-group variance to fit a slope, meaning the apparent trend is driven almost entirely by two data points at different temperatures and different substrates. The Si-substrate samples (Rows 8–9, 25°C, Tc = 1.41 K) introduce a substrate confound that cannot be separated from the deposition temperature effect with the current data. Post-deposition surface treatments (wet etch, H2O2, dry etch, HF) also vary across samples at the same deposition temperature, adding further noise. The seed correlation for this hypothesis originates from a Ta-Hf alloy system (Yang_2026), and the physical mechanism — potentially phase segregation or elemental redistribution — does not transfer straightforwardly to elemental Al films.
writeup.alternative_explanations: Substrate identity (Si vs. c-Al2O3) is the most plausible dominant variable: differences in lattice mismatch, thermal expansion coefficient, and epitaxial strain between substrates likely drive the largest Tc variation observed, with deposition temperature playing at most a secondary role modulated through grain structure and transport regime.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is low (0.25) due to: (1) only 9 total samples with 6 clustered at identical deposition conditions (200°C, c-Al2O3), providing minimal statistical leverage; (2) the two highest-Tc samples are on a different substrate, making substrate the primary confound; (3) the Tc range is narrow (1.05–1.41 K) and partly attributable to clean vs. dirty transport regime rather than deposition temperature; (4) the seed correlation derives from a physically distinct material system (Ta-Hf alloy); and (5) this assessment is consistent with a prior evaluation at 0.20 confidence — the new data adds marginal coherence within the sapphire subset but does not resolve the substrate confound.
writeup.qrem_implications: A deposition-temperature–Tc mapping function for Al is not supportable from current data. Any QREM layer attempting to predict Tc from deposition temperature for Al would require at minimum a substrate-stratified model, and even then the within-substrate data (c-Al2O3 only, 7 samples at two temperatures) is insufficient to fit a reliable functional form. If a mapping is eventually warranted, it would likely need to be a joint function of deposition temperature and substrate type — or substrate-specific lookup tables — rather than a single scalar relationship. Until controlled experiments on a single substrate across a temperature range are available, this variable should not be used as a predictive input in the QREM materials-to-device mapping layer for Al.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Re-analysis should be restricted to Al on c-Al2O3 as a focused sub-hypothesis, since that is the only substrate with more than one deposition temperature represented (7 samples: 6 at 200°C, 1 at -72°C). However, even this subset is underpowered — a meaningful re-analysis requires at least 3–4 distinct deposition temperatures on the same substrate. Al on Si has only 2 samples at a single temperature and cannot support a trend analysis. No other materials in the current corpus have sufficient Al samples to warrant a separate focused re-analysis.
questions_for_reviewer: Is the dirty-limit classification of the cryogenic sample (Row 3, -72°C) confirmed by independent transport measurements (e.g., RRR or sheet resistance), and if so, should the Tc difference between Row 3 and the 200°C samples be attributed primarily to transport regime rather than deposition temperature?

Are the Si-substrate samples (Rows 8–9) considered physically comparable to the c-Al2O3 samples for the purpose of this hypothesis, or should they be excluded from the deposition-temperature–Tc analysis on the grounds that substrate is a categorical variable that must be held constant?

Do any of the Hedrick_2026 samples share identical post-deposition surface treatments, allowing surface treatment to be controlled for when comparing deposition temperatures within the c-Al2O3 subset?

Is there a physical mechanism specific to elemental Al — such as grain boundary density or void formation as a function of deposition temperature — that would predict a non-monotonic Tc response, consistent with the observed pattern where room-temperature Si depositions yield higher Tc than either cryogenic or elevated-temperature c-Al2O3 depositions?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value experiment would be a controlled deposition series of Al films on a single substrate (either c-Al2O3 or Si) across at least five deposition temperatures spanning -72°C to 200°C, with identical film thickness, deposition rate, and post-deposition treatment, followed by simultaneous measurement of Tc, RRR, and grain structure (XRD). This would directly test whether a monotonic, non-monotonic, or flat Tc–T_dep relationship exists within a single substrate system and would allow transport regime effects to be separated from direct thermal deposition effects.

writeup.title: Sheet resistance and internal quality factor in NbSe2 thin films show a non-monotonic, scattered relationship that does not support a simple disorder-loss model
writeup.hypothesis: Higher sheet resistance (Ohm/sq) in NbSe2 thin films is associated with lower internal quality factor (Qi_internal), suggesting that increased disorder or proximity to the superconductor-insulator transition degrades microwave loss.
writeup.summary: Across 6 NbSe2 samples from a single study (Zaman_2025), the relationship between sheet resistance and Qi_internal is non-monotonic and highly scattered: the peak Qi of 60,000 occurs at an intermediate sheet resistance (~60 Ohm/sq), while both the highest-disorder sample (240 Ohm/sq, Qi=8,000) and the lowest-disorder sample (10 Ohm/sq, Qi=7,000) show similarly poor performance. This pattern directly contradicts a simple monotonic disorder-loss hypothesis and is consistent with the prior inconclusive assessment (confidence 0.2) for this pair. The data are insufficient to support, refute, or parameterize any directional mapping between sheet resistance and Qi in this material system.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: The highest sheet resistance sample (D1, 240 Ohm/sq, dirty-limit) does exhibit one of the lowest Qi values (8,000), and the highest Qi (60,000) occurs at a lower sheet resistance (D2, 60 Ohm/sq), which is directionally consistent with disorder-driven loss at the high-Rs extreme.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: The lowest sheet resistance sample (D8, 10 Ohm/sq, clean-limit) has a Qi of only 7,000 — as low as the dirtiest sample — and two samples at nearly identical sheet resistance (D3 at 29 Ohm/sq, Qi=7,000; D4 at 25 Ohm/sq, Qi=24,000) differ by more than 3x in Qi, indicating that uncontrolled variables dominate over sheet resistance.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: A denser sampling of the 30–200 Ohm/sq range with independent structural characterization (e.g., RRR, XRD rocking curve, TEM) to decouple sheet resistance from crystalline quality, film thickness, and interface effects.

writeup.finding_detail: The six Zaman_2025 NbSe2 samples, ordered by sheet resistance, yield Qi values of: 240 Ohm/sq → 8,000; 60 Ohm/sq → 60,000; 29 Ohm/sq → 7,000; 25 Ohm/sq → 24,000; 19 Ohm/sq → 12,000; 10 Ohm/sq → 7,000. This distribution is peaked at intermediate Rs and collapses at both extremes, inconsistent with any monotonic disorder-loss model. The near-identical Qi values of D1 (highest Rs) and D8 (lowest Rs) suggest that different loss mechanisms may dominate at the two ends of the disorder spectrum — possibly quasiparticle loss near the SIT for D1 and interface or TLS loss in the clean-limit D8 — but this is speculative given the data available. The large Qi spread between D3 and D4 at nearly the same sheet resistance (~25–29 Ohm/sq) further implies that sheet resistance alone is a poor predictor of microwave loss in this vdW material. Notably, the Böttcher_2025 NbN result (Qi independent of normal-state resistance over the range studied) suggests that the Qi-Rs relationship may be material-specific and non-universal, adding further caution against generalizing any trend observed here.
writeup.alternative_explanations: Qi in NbSe2 vdW films is likely more strongly controlled by film thickness, layer count, crystalline quality, or substrate coupling than by sheet resistance per se; the non-monotonic behavior may reflect a morphological sweet spot at intermediate thickness rather than a disorder-loss mechanism, and stacking faults or grain boundaries intrinsic to vdW growth may decouple the usual disorder-Qi relationship valid for conventional amorphous or polycrystalline thin films.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Only 6 samples, all from a single study on a single material, with no independent replication. The data show a non-monotonic, high-scatter relationship rather than a directional trend, and the prior approved finding for this exact pair was already rated 0.2/inconclusive. The sample count is too small to distinguish a true peaked relationship from random scatter, and the absence of independent structural characterization prevents attribution of Qi variation to sheet resistance versus confounding microstructural variables. No upward revision from the prior assessment is warranted.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM mapping function of the form Qi = f(Rs) is not supportable for NbSe2 with current evidence. The non-monotonic, scattered data preclude fitting any simple monotonic function (e.g., power-law or exponential decay), and the large Qi variance at similar Rs values (~3x spread at 25–29 Ohm/sq) means that sheet resistance alone would be a poor predictor input even if more data were available. If a mapping is eventually warranted, it would likely need to be non-monotonic (e.g., peaked or piecewise) and conditioned on additional covariates such as film thickness or crystalline quality metrics. For now, sheet resistance should not be used as a standalone predictor of Qi in the QREM materials-to-device layer for vdW superconductors.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Re-analysis per material class is warranted but not yet feasible. NbSe2 is the only material represented, with only 6 samples from a single study — insufficient for a robust per-material regression or trend confirmation. NbN (Böttcher_2025) has a separate result suggesting no Qi-Rs correlation, but the sample overlap with this hypothesis corpus is unclear. A focused re-analysis of NbSe2 specifically should be triggered once at least 10–15 samples with independent structural characterization are available. No other vdW superconductor (NbS2, TaS2) currently has enough samples in the corpus to warrant separate analysis.
questions_for_reviewer: Are the Zaman_2025 NbSe2 samples (D1–D8) fabricated with the same resonator geometry and substrate, or do geometric and substrate differences between samples contribute to the observed Qi scatter independent of sheet resistance?

Is the clean-limit sample D8 (10 Ohm/sq, Qi=7,000) anomalously low due to a known fabrication issue (e.g., oxidation, delamination, or proximity effect from substrate), or is this Qi value considered reliable by the authors?

Does the Zaman_2025 study report power-dependent Qi measurements that would allow separation of TLS loss (dominant at low photon number) from quasiparticle loss (dominant near Tc or at high disorder), which would clarify whether different loss mechanisms operate at the two ends of the Rs range?

Given that D3 (29 Ohm/sq) and D4 (25 Ohm/sq) have nearly identical sheet resistance but Qi values of 7,000 and 24,000 respectively, do the authors attribute this discrepancy to a specific structural or fabrication variable, or is it unexplained in the source paper?
writeup.suggested_followup: The most valuable next step is a systematic NbSe2 thin-film study that independently varies sheet resistance while controlling for film thickness and crystalline quality (verified by RRR and XRD), combined with power-dependent Qi measurements to identify the dominant loss mechanism at each disorder level — this would directly test whether the apparent Qi peak at intermediate Rs is a reproducible morphological sweet spot or a sampling artifact of the current 6-sample dataset.

writeup.title: No robust monotonic relationship between sheet resistance and internal quality factor is observed across six NbSe2 thin-film samples
writeup.hypothesis: In NbSe2 thin films, higher sheet resistance (Ohm/sq) is associated with lower internal quality factor (Qi_internal), suggesting that increased disorder or proximity to the superconductor-insulator transition degrades microwave loss.
writeup.summary: Examination of six NbSe2 samples from Zaman_2025 reveals no consistent monotonic relationship between sheet resistance and Qi_internal. The best-performing sample (D2, Qi=60,000) sits at an intermediate sheet resistance of 60 Ohm/sq, outperforming all lower sheet resistance samples including the clean-limit sample D8 (10 Ohm/sq, Qi=7,000), which directly contradicts the hypothesis. The only weak support is that the highest sheet resistance outlier (D1, 240 Ohm/sq) does show low Qi (8,000), but this single data point is insufficient to establish a trend given the non-monotonic scatter across the remaining samples.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: Sample D1 (240 Ohm/sq, Qi=8,000) is consistent with disorder-driven loss at extreme sheet resistance, and the broad D4-D5-D8 sub-range shows loosely increasing Qi with increasing sheet resistance, weakly consistent with the hypothesis over a narrow window.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Sample D2 (60 Ohm/sq) achieves the highest Qi in the dataset (60,000) despite intermediate sheet resistance, and the clean-limit sample D8 (10 Ohm/sq) yields one of the lowest Qi values (7,000), both of which directly falsify a simple monotonic negative correlation between sheet resistance and Qi_internal.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: A larger, systematically controlled dataset (>20 samples) with fixed film thickness and deposition conditions spanning a continuous range of sheet resistance values, combined with TLS loss tangent and power-dependent Qi measurements to identify the dominant loss mechanism per sample.

writeup.finding_detail: Across the six Zaman_2025 NbSe2 samples, sheet resistance spans 10–240 Ohm/sq and Qi_internal spans 7,000–60,000, but the two quantities do not co-vary monotonically. The clean-limit sample D8 (10 Ohm/sq) and the intermediate sample D3 (29 Ohm/sq) both yield Qi~7,000, the lowest in the set, while D2 (60 Ohm/sq) achieves Qi=60,000—the highest—despite having higher sheet resistance than D3, D4, D5, and D8. D4 (25 Ohm/sq, Qi=24,000) and D5 (19 Ohm/sq, Qi=12,000) further illustrate that Qi does not track inversely with sheet resistance in the low-to-intermediate range. Only D1 (240 Ohm/sq, Qi=8,000) is consistent with the hypothesis, but a single extreme outlier cannot establish a trend. The overall pattern is dominated by scatter, suggesting that unmeasured covariates—likely film thickness, transport regime, interface quality, or structural disorder—are the primary determinants of Qi in this sample set.
writeup.alternative_explanations: Transport regime (clean vs. dirty limit) and film thickness likely co-vary with sheet resistance across these samples and may independently control Qi through regime-specific loss mechanisms (e.g., vortex dynamics in the clean limit, quasiparticle losses in the dirty limit), confounding any direct sheet resistance–Qi relationship. Fabrication variability in grain size, surface roughness, and stoichiometry may additionally dominate Qi differences, consistent with the Böttcher_2025 null result in NbN where Qi was found to be independent of sheet resistance near the superconductor-insulator transition.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is low (0.2) due to the small sample size (N=6), single-study provenance (all from Zaman_2025), non-monotonic scatter that directly contradicts the hypothesis for the majority of samples, and the absence of controlled covariates (thickness, deposition conditions). The best-performing sample violates the hypothesis, and the clean-limit sample with the lowest sheet resistance performs among the worst. This matches the prior approved confidence rating of 0.2 (inconclusive), and closer inspection of D3 and D8 does not improve the case. The independent Böttcher_2025 null result in NbN provides additional prior reason to expect no simple correlation in superconducting thin films near the SIT.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM mapping function of the form Qi_internal = f(sheet_resistance) is not supportable for NbSe2 at this time. The data do not justify even a monotonic functional form, let alone a quantitative one. If a mapping layer were forced, the only defensible statement is a weak flag that sheet resistance >200 Ohm/sq may be associated with degraded Qi (Qi<10,000), but this is based on a single sample and should not be encoded as a reliable predictor. Until controlled experiments decouple sheet resistance from film thickness and transport regime, any QREM node linking sheet resistance to Qi_internal for NbSe2 should carry a high uncertainty flag and be treated as uninformative for device selection purposes.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Re-analysis focused on NbSe2 is warranted but currently underpowered: all six samples are from a single study (Zaman_2025) and do not span a controlled parameter space. No other material in this corpus has both fields measured, so cross-material comparison is not yet possible. A focused re-analysis of NbSe2 should be triggered only when a second independent dataset with ≥10 samples and documented film thickness per sample becomes available. NbN (Böttcher_2025) represents a separate material system with a documented null result and should be tracked independently if sheet resistance data become available in that corpus.
questions_for_reviewer: Are film thicknesses documented for each of the six Zaman_2025 NbSe2 samples (D1–D8)? If so, can sheet resistance be decomposed into resistivity × thickness to assess whether resistivity or thickness is the dominant covariate with Qi?

Is the transport regime classification (clean/dirty/intermediate) for these samples based on measured mean free path and coherence length, or inferred from sheet resistance alone? This distinction is critical for interpreting D8's anomalously low Qi despite its clean-limit designation.

Does the Zaman_2025 study report any structural characterization (XRD, AFM roughness, stoichiometry) that could explain why D2 (60 Ohm/sq) achieves Qi=60,000 while D3 (29 Ohm/sq) achieves only Qi=7,000 despite lower sheet resistance?

Should the single author-stated correlation seeding this hypothesis be treated as a qualitative claim or a quantitative fit result? If it is a fit, what functional form and R² were reported, and over which sample subset?
writeup.suggested_followup: The most valuable next step is a systematic NbSe2 thin-film deposition series in which film thickness is held fixed while sheet resistance is varied through controlled disorder introduction (e.g., ion irradiation or controlled deposition rate), combined with power-dependent Qi measurements and TLS loss tangent extraction to identify the dominant loss mechanism. This would decouple sheet resistance from thickness as a covariate and directly test whether the sheet resistance–Qi relationship is intrinsic or confounded by fabrication variability.

At 20 nm, Tc = 12.0 K, higher than the 5 nm sample, supporting the idea that thicker films above the ultra-thin limit recover higher Tc.

This directly contradicts a simple positive thickness-Tc monotonic relationship. Critically, this sample received a 1000°C anneal, which is a major confound: prior findings establish that higher annealing/deposition temperature is associated with lower Tc in superconducting thin films, potentially explaining the lower Tc independent of thickness.

writeup.title: Film thickness alone does not predict Tc in NbN ALD thin films across labs: a 4-sample dataset yields no monotonic trend and is dominated by processing confounds
writeup.hypothesis: In NbN thin films, film thickness is meaningfully correlated with superconducting critical temperature (Tc), such that thinner films exhibit lower Tc due to thickness-dependent suppression of superconductivity.
writeup.summary: Across 4 NbN samples spanning 5–50 nm, no monotonic thickness–Tc relationship is observed: Tc peaks at 12.0 K for 20 nm films (Böttcher_2025) and is equally low at 10.0 K for both the thinnest (5 nm, Wu_2026_M2M) and thickest (50 nm, Wu_2026_M2O) films. The thickest sample received a 1000°C anneal, a known independent suppressor of Tc, making it impossible to attribute its lower Tc to thickness. Processing conditions, substrate choice, and lab-to-lab deposition variability appear to be at least as influential as thickness in this dataset, and no mapping function between thickness and Tc is supportable at this time.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: The 5 nm film (Wu_2026_M2M, Tc = 10.0 K) shows lower Tc than the 20 nm films (Böttcher_2025, Tc = 12.0 K), which is physically consistent with ultra-thin-limit suppression of superconductivity in NbN — a well-documented phenomenon in the literature.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: The thickest film (Wu_2026_M2O, 50 nm) has Tc = 10.0 K, equal to the thinnest film and lower than the intermediate-thickness samples, directly contradicting a monotonic trend; this sample also received a 1000°C anneal, a major confound independently associated with Tc suppression in superconducting thin films.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: A controlled thickness series (e.g., 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 nm) from a single deposition run on identical substrates with no post-deposition annealing variation is the minimum experiment needed to isolate thickness effects on Tc in NbN.

writeup.finding_detail: The 4 NbN samples span three distinct experimental contexts: Wu_2026_M2M (5 nm, silicon substrate, Tc = 10.0 K), Böttcher_2025_NbN_CPW and Böttcher_2025_Planaron (both 20 nm, annealed sapphire, Tc = 12.0 K), and Wu_2026_M2O (50 nm, AlN-on-sapphire, 1000°C anneal, Tc = 10.0 K). The non-monotonic pattern — with Tc highest at intermediate thickness — is inconsistent with a simple thickness-suppression model. The 1000°C anneal applied to Wu_2026_M2O is a critical confound: prior findings in this corpus establish that elevated deposition or annealing temperature independently suppresses Tc, and this effect cannot be disentangled from any thickness contribution in the current data. Additionally, the Böttcher_2025_Planaron sample uses local ALE thinning to create a weak link, so its reported 20 nm thickness reflects the bulk film rather than the functional device region, reducing its direct comparability. The single author-stated correlation seed (Chang_2025) describes an inverse proximity effect in Au-encapsulated Ta, a mechanistically distinct phenomenon that does not generalize to intrinsic NbN thickness-Tc suppression. Taken together, the dataset is too small, too confounded, and too heterogeneous to support or refute the hypothesis.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that Tc variation in this dataset is primarily driven by thermal processing history (annealing temperature) and substrate-dependent film growth (stoichiometry, grain structure, interfacial disorder) rather than thickness per se — consistent with the known sensitivity of NbN Tc to deposition conditions and the independently established finding that higher anneal temperature suppresses Tc in this corpus.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.2 (very low) due to: (1) only 4 samples, all from different labs and substrates, providing minimal statistical power; (2) a critical uncontrolled confound — the 1000°C anneal on the thickest sample — that independently explains its lower Tc; (3) the non-monotonic pattern in the data, which is inconsistent with the directional hypothesis; (4) the mechanistic irrelevance of the single author-stated seed correlation (Au/Ta proximity effect vs. NbN intrinsic thickness suppression); and (5) the absence of any within-lab, within-run thickness series that would be required to isolate thickness as a variable.
writeup.qrem_implications: A thickness-to-Tc mapping function for NbN is not supportable from this dataset. For QREM's materials-to-device mapping layer, this means film thickness cannot currently be used as a reliable predictor of Tc for NbN, and any model that encodes a monotonic thickness–Tc relationship for NbN would be unsupported and potentially misleading. If a mapping function were eventually warranted (pending controlled data), it would likely need to be non-linear, bounded below by an ultra-thin-limit suppression regime (plausibly below ~10 nm), and would require processing condition (anneal temperature, deposition method) and substrate as co-variates rather than treating thickness as an independent predictor. Until such data exist, QREM should treat NbN Tc as primarily determined by processing conditions, with thickness as a secondary and context-dependent modifier.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: NbN is the only material in this corpus with co-measured thickness and Tc, but 4 samples is insufficient for a meaningful per-material re-analysis. A focused re-analysis of NbN specifically would be warranted only after ingesting additional samples — ideally from controlled thickness series within single deposition runs. No other material in the current corpus has enough co-measured thickness–Tc pairs to support a parallel analysis. If NbTiN or TaN samples with both fields are added in future corpus updates, a comparative multi-material analysis would be valuable.
questions_for_reviewer: Is the 1000°C anneal applied to Wu_2026_M2O (50 nm NbN on AlN/sapphire) documented as a post-deposition step or as the substrate pre-treatment temperature? If it is a substrate anneal rather than a film anneal, the magnitude of its effect on NbN Tc may differ from what is assumed here — please clarify the thermal history of this sample.

For the Böttcher_2025_Planaron sample, is the reported Tc of 12.0 K measured on the bulk 20 nm film region or on the ALE-thinned weak-link region? If the latter, the effective thickness at the measurement point may be substantially less than 20 nm, which would change the interpretation of this data point.

Are there additional NbN samples in the source papers (Wu_2026, Böttcher_2025) with different thicknesses that were not ingested into the corpus — for example, control films or thickness calibration samples — that could expand the dataset without requiring new experiments?

The literature seed (Chang_2025) is flagged as mechanistically distinct (Au/Ta proximity effect). Is there a more directly relevant NbN thickness–Tc literature source that should be used to seed or contextualize this hypothesis, such as Semenov et al. or Engel et al. on NbN film thickness series?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next step is to identify and ingest published NbN thickness series from single-lab, single-run deposition studies (e.g., ALD or reactive sputtering series on sapphire or silicon) where thickness is systematically varied while all other parameters are held constant and Tc is directly measured — several such datasets exist in the SNSPD and superconducting resonator literature. This would allow a within-run thickness–Tc curve to be established for NbN without the cross-lab confounds present in the current corpus, and would directly test whether the ultra-thin-limit suppression observed between 5 nm and 20 nm is reproducible and whether Tc saturates at a bulk value above ~20 nm.

writeup.title: Hypothesis that Al film thickness correlates with Tc is untestable in the current corpus due to zero variance in film_thickness_nm across all 9 samples.
writeup.hypothesis: In Al superconducting thin films, film thickness is meaningfully correlated with superconducting critical temperature (Tc), such that variations in film_thickness_nm produce measurable changes in Tc_K.
writeup.summary: All 9 Al film samples in the corpus (Hedrick_2026, Table II) were deposited at exactly 200.0 nm, providing zero variance in the proposed independent variable and rendering the thickness-Tc correlation hypothesis completely untestable with available data. Tc does vary across samples (1.05–1.41 K), but this variation is attributable to substrate identity (c-Al2O3 vs. Si) and transport regime (clean vs. dirty limit), not to any thickness difference. This is a null-data result: not a refutation of the hypothesis, but a finding that the current corpus was not designed to probe it.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: There is no supporting evidence for the hypothesis in this corpus. No sample-to-sample thickness variation exists, so no thickness-dependent Tc trend can be identified or even approximated.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: The strongest complicating evidence is the pair of Si-substrate samples (Hedrick_2026 Rows 8 and 9) showing Tc = 1.41 K versus sapphire-substrate samples showing Tc as low as 1.05 K — all at identical 200.0 nm thickness — demonstrating that substrate and surface treatment, not thickness, drive Tc variation in this dataset. The seed correlation (Chang_2025) concerns a Ta+Au proximity-effect bilayer mechanism, which is physically distinct from intrinsic single-layer Al thickness dependence and should not be assumed to generalize.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Al film samples spanning a wide thickness range (e.g., 5–500 nm) on a fixed substrate under controlled deposition conditions are the critical missing data; ultra-thin Al films (<20 nm) are especially important as quantum size effects and surface scattering are expected to be most pronounced in that regime.

writeup.finding_detail: The Hedrick_2026 dataset contains 9 Al thin-film samples (Table II, Rows 1–9) that are uniform at 200.0 nm thickness by experimental design — the study was not intended to probe thickness dependence. Tc_K ranges from 1.05 K (Row 1, wet-etch, c-Al2O3, clean_limit) to 1.41 K (Rows 8 and 9, 49% HF 10s etch, Si substrate, dirty_limit), a spread of 0.36 K that is fully explained by substrate identity and transport regime without invoking any thickness effect. The replicate pair (Rows 8 and 9, both Tc = 1.41 K at 200.0 nm) confirms that the elevated Tc on Si is a reproducible substrate/surface-chemistry effect. Row 3 (cryo etch, c-Al2O3, dirty_limit, Tc = 1.15 K) sits at an intermediate value consistent with dirty-limit disorder enhancement on sapphire rather than any thickness-driven mechanism. Because film_thickness_nm is a constant across all records, no statistical or qualitative assessment of a thickness-Tc relationship is possible; this is a fundamental experimental design constraint, not a marginal data quality issue.
writeup.alternative_explanations: Substrate identity (Si vs. c-Al2O3) and transport regime (dirty vs. clean limit, modulated by surface treatment and etching chemistry) are the primary drivers of Tc variation in this dataset and provide a fully sufficient explanation for the observed 1.05–1.41 K spread without invoking film thickness. The seed literature claim (Chang_2025) involves a proximity-effect mechanism from a normal-metal Au overlayer on Ta, which is physically distinct from intrinsic Al film thickness dependence and should not be used as prior support for this hypothesis.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at the floor (0.1) because the hypothesis is structurally untestable with this corpus: the independent variable (film_thickness_nm) has exactly zero variance across all 9 samples. A confidence above the floor would require at least some thickness variation to evaluate, even weakly. The single author-stated correlation seed derives from a physically distinct system (Ta+Au bilayer, proximity effect) and does not constitute evidence for intrinsic Al thickness dependence. No amount of statistical analysis on this dataset can yield a meaningful estimate of the thickness-Tc relationship in Al.
writeup.qrem_implications: No thickness-to-Tc mapping function for Al is supportable from this corpus. For the QREM materials-to-device mapping layer, film_thickness_nm cannot currently be used as a predictive input variable for Al Tc: there is no empirical basis to parameterize such a function, and the single-point thickness value (200 nm) provides no leverage to estimate slope, curvature, or threshold behavior. If a mapping function of the form Tc = f(thickness) is required for Al in QREM, it must be populated from external literature (e.g., studies of Al films from 5–200 nm) or from targeted new experiments, and should be flagged as externally sourced rather than corpus-derived. Substrate identity and transport regime are currently the better-supported input variables for predicting Al Tc within this corpus.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: This hypothesis should not be re-run on the current Al corpus until thickness-varied Al samples are added — re-analysis of the existing 9 samples will yield the same null result by construction. No other material in the current corpus has been flagged as having sufficient thickness-varied samples to warrant a focused re-analysis of this specific hypothesis. If Ta samples with varying thickness exist elsewhere in the corpus (as suggested by the prior finding [derived_BCS_gap_meV_vs_film_thickness_nm__ta]), a Ta-specific re-analysis of directly measured Tc_K (not BCS-derived gap) vs. thickness would be the most informative parallel investigation, but should be treated as a separate hypothesis.
questions_for_reviewer: Are there Al thin-film studies in the broader literature (e.g., Chubov et al., Court et al., or more recent qubit fabrication papers) that systematically vary Al thickness from <20 nm to >200 nm and report Tc, which could be ingested to populate this hypothesis with real thickness variance?

Is the 200 nm Al thickness in Hedrick_2026 a deliberate fixed parameter chosen to isolate surface/substrate effects, and if so, does the study design explicitly exclude thickness as a variable — confirming that no thickness-Tc inference was intended by the authors?

Given that dirty-limit transport regime (driven by surface treatment) raises Tc in this dataset, should the QREM mapping layer treat transport regime (clean vs. dirty limit) as a first-class input variable for Al Tc prediction, independent of thickness?

The seed correlation count is listed as 1 (from Chang_2025, a Ta+Au proximity-effect system). Should this seed be retired or re-tagged as 'mechanism-distinct' to prevent it from continuing to generate Al thickness-Tc hypotheses in future reasoning cycles?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next step is targeted ingestion of Al thin-film studies that vary thickness as a primary experimental parameter — specifically studies covering the 5–100 nm range where quantum size effects and surface scattering are expected to produce measurable Tc suppression. A secondary analysis comparing dirty-limit vs. clean-limit Tc across the existing 9 samples (with substrate as a covariate) would extract the maximum available signal from the current corpus and could yield a positive or negative finding on the substrate/transport-regime hypothesis, which is the actual driver of Tc variation here.

writeup.title: Hypothesis that PtSi critical temperature varies with film thickness cannot be evaluated from available data due to complete absence of thickness variation across all six samples.
writeup.hypothesis: There is a meaningful connection between superconducting critical temperature (Tc_K) and film thickness (film_thickness_nm) in PtSi thin films, such that varying thickness leads to systematic changes in Tc.
writeup.summary: All six PtSi samples in the corpus (Nanayakkara_2026) share identical values of Tc_K = 0.9 K and film_thickness_nm = 25.0 nm, yielding zero variance in both variables and rendering any correlation analysis statistically degenerate. The dataset represents a single point in (thickness, Tc) space rather than a distribution, so neither a positive nor a null thickness-dependence can be established. This is an informative negative result about the scope of the available data, not about the underlying physics.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: There is no supporting evidence for a Tc–thickness relationship in PtSi; no sample pair exists with differing thickness values that could demonstrate even a directional trend.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: All six samples (ASQ-50-100, SSQ-25, SSQ-50, ScS-100, ScS-25, ScS-50) appear to be device geometry variants fabricated from the same 25 nm PtSi film batch, meaning the study was not designed to probe thickness dependence and the sample name suffixes reflect qubit or resonator geometry rather than film thickness. The seeding correlation cited from Chang_2025 involves Au encapsulation modulating a proximity-effect gap in Ta bilayers — a physically distinct mechanism that does not transfer directly to single-layer PtSi.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: PtSi samples with systematically varied film thickness (e.g., 5, 10, 15, 25, 50, 100 nm), each with independently measured Tc_K, are the minimum requirement to evaluate this hypothesis; no such data exist in the current corpus.

writeup.finding_detail: The six PtSi records from Nanayakkara_2026 — ASQ-50-100, SSQ-25, SSQ-50, ScS-100, ScS-25, and ScS-50 — all report Tc_K = 0.9 K and film_thickness_nm = 25.0 nm without exception. Because both variables are constant, no statistical measure of association (Pearson r, Spearman ρ, or otherwise) is computable; the data collapse to a single point. The study design appears to hold film thickness fixed while varying device geometry, which is scientifically valid for its intended purpose but provides no leverage on thickness-dependent Tc behavior. The value of 0.9 K at 25 nm on high-resistivity Si is itself a useful process anchor for PtSi under these deposition and annealing conditions, but it cannot be extrapolated to other thicknesses without additional data. The seeding claim from Chang_2025 concerns a bilayer proximity-effect scenario in Ta/Au stacks and is not physically analogous to PtSi single-layer thickness variation, further weakening the basis for expecting a strong effect in this material.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible interpretation is that the Nanayakkara_2026 samples are device geometry variants from a single wafer run, making identical Tc and thickness expected by design rather than coincidental. It is also possible that PtSi Tc is genuinely insensitive to thickness in the range near 25 nm — a known behavior in some silicide superconductors where the fully reacted stoichiometric phase dominates — but this cannot be confirmed or refuted from the present corpus.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set to the minimum meaningful level (0.1) because the dataset is statistically degenerate: zero variance in both Tc_K and film_thickness_nm across all six samples makes it impossible to compute or infer any correlation. The single author-stated correlation originates from a physically distinct system (Ta/Au bilayer) and does not constitute independent evidence for PtSi. No prior approved findings address PtSi thickness dependence. The 0.1 reflects that the hypothesis is not falsified in principle — it simply cannot be evaluated with the data at hand.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM materials-to-device mapping function of the form Tc = f(thickness) for PtSi is not supportable from this corpus. The data establish only a single operating point (25 nm → 0.9 K) under one set of process conditions, which is insufficient to parameterize even a linear mapping, let alone capture any non-monotonic or threshold behavior that thin-film superconductors commonly exhibit. Until thickness-varied PtSi data are available, any QREM layer using PtSi should treat Tc as a fixed material constant (0.9 K) rather than a thickness-tunable parameter, and should flag this assumption explicitly as unvalidated.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: This hypothesis should not be re-run for PtSi until new thickness-varied samples are added to the corpus; the current six samples add no new information beyond what a single sample would provide. For context, the related film-thickness hypotheses for NbSe2 and Ta were flagged as derived-field artifacts (BCS gap computed from Tc), so those materials also do not currently offer a clean direct Tc-vs-thickness test. No material in the present corpus has sufficient thickness-varied samples with directly measured Tc to warrant a focused re-analysis of this specific hypothesis.
questions_for_reviewer: Does the Nanayakkara_2026 study report any PtSi samples at thicknesses other than 25 nm in supplementary materials or companion publications that were not captured in the corpus extraction — specifically, were thickness series fabricated but not included in the primary dataset?

Is the 0.9 K Tc value for 25 nm PtSi consistent with the broader PtSi literature (bulk Tc ~0.9–1.0 K), and if so, does this suggest that PtSi Tc is already near its bulk limit at 25 nm, making thickness dependence unlikely to be detectable above this thickness?

Should the seeding correlation from Chang_2025 (Au encapsulation on Ta) be retired as a seed for PtSi hypotheses given the physical dissimilarity of the systems, or is there a theoretical argument for why proximity-effect thickness scaling should generalize to single-layer silicides?

Are the sample name suffixes (-25, -50, -100 in ScS and SSQ series) confirmed to refer to qubit junction or resonator dimensions rather than film thickness, and is there any risk that a future corpus update could incorrectly re-interpret these as thickness labels?
writeup.suggested_followup: The most valuable next step is to identify or commission a PtSi thickness series — ideally 5 to 100 nm in at least five steps — deposited and annealed under identical conditions on the same substrate type, with Tc measured by transport for each thickness. This would directly test whether PtSi Tc is thickness-sensitive in the technologically relevant range and would provide the minimum dataset needed to parameterize a QREM mapping function. As a lower-cost alternative, a systematic literature search for PtSi thin-film studies reporting both thickness and Tc could rapidly expand the corpus without new fabrication.

writeup.title: Re thin film Tc vs. deposition temperature is untestable from current corpus due to complete absence of experimental variation in deposition conditions
writeup.hypothesis: For Re thin films deposited by DC magnetron sputtering, higher deposition temperature is associated with a lower superconducting critical temperature (Tc).
writeup.summary: All 6 Re corpus samples (Wang_2026) were deposited at a single temperature (900°C) and exhibit a single Tc value (1.9 K), providing zero variance in either variable and making it mathematically impossible to assess any directional relationship. This is not a negative result — it reflects a gap in experimental coverage rather than evidence that Re Tc is insensitive to deposition temperature. The finding is entirely uninformative for evaluating the Re-specific hypothesis and should not be interpreted as supporting or refuting it.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: There is no supporting evidence for the hypothesis. No sample in the corpus was deposited at a temperature other than 900°C, so no trend can be identified.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Five of the six samples (Wang_2026_Transmon_1 through Transmon_5) received a 1200°C post-deposition substrate anneal — a thermal treatment substantially exceeding the deposition temperature itself — which is a major uncontrolled confound that could independently set or reset Tc and is not captured in the deposition_temperature_C field. Additionally, the observed Tc of 1.9 K is consistent with the bulk Re value, suggesting the deposition conditions used here may already yield near-bulk behavior.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Re films deposited across a range of temperatures (e.g., 600–1000°C) from the same deposition system and substrate, with Tc measured for each condition and post-deposition annealing either absent or held constant, are required to test this hypothesis.

writeup.finding_detail: The entire Re corpus derives from a single publication (Wang_2026) using a single deposition temperature of 900°C, yielding Tc = 1.9 K across all six samples regardless of device architecture (bare film vs. transmon with Al/AlOx junction). Because both variables are invariant, no correlation coefficient or directional trend can be computed — this is a degenerate dataset for the purpose of hypothesis testing. Notably, Wang_2026_Transmon_1 through Transmon_5 all underwent a 1200°C substrate anneal after deposition, a thermal step that could dominate any deposition-temperature effect on crystallinity and Tc; this confound is not separable from deposition temperature effects in the current data. The Tc of 1.9 K is consistent with bulk Re (bulk Tc ~1.69–1.97 K depending on purity and strain), suggesting that at 900°C deposition, Re films may already approach bulk-like behavior, but this cannot be confirmed without lower-temperature comparison points. The seed correlation cited (n_stated_by_authors = 1) originates from a Ta-Hf study and cannot be transferred to Re without independent Re-specific evidence.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that Re, as a refractory metal, may exhibit weak sensitivity of Tc to deposition temperature within the accessible sputtering range, with Tc pinned near the bulk value; however, this cannot be distinguished from a scenario where strong sensitivity exists but was simply never tested, given that only one deposition temperature appears in the corpus.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.1 (minimal, not zero) solely to acknowledge that the observed Tc of 1.9 K is physically consistent with bulk Re and that no anomalous Tc enhancement or suppression is apparent at 900°C. However, the dataset is a single experimental point with zero variance in both variables, making any correlation assessment impossible. The low confidence reflects the complete absence of usable evidence rather than weak evidence — the data is uninformative, not contradictory.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM deposition-temperature-to-Tc mapping function for Re is not supportable from current data. To construct even a minimal mapping function (e.g., a linear or empirical fit of Tc vs. T_dep for Re), at least 3–5 distinct deposition temperatures with measured Tc values would be required. The current corpus provides only a single anchor point (T_dep = 900°C, Tc = 1.9 K), which can at best serve as a fixed-point constraint in a future mapping function but cannot define its slope or functional form. Until multi-temperature Re data are available, any QREM layer using Re should treat Tc as a fixed material constant (~1.9 K) rather than a deposition-tunable parameter, with the caveat that this assumption is untested across deposition conditions.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: This hypothesis should be re-run per material class once multi-temperature data are available. Within the current corpus, no Re sub-analysis is warranted — the single deposition condition makes it uninformative. Other refractory superconductors in the corpus (e.g., Ta, Nb, Mo) should be checked for whether they have sufficient deposition-temperature variation to serve as analogues or to motivate targeted Re experiments. If Ta or Nb show a deposition-temperature/Tc trend, that would strengthen the scientific rationale for acquiring the missing Re data.
questions_for_reviewer: Is the 1200°C post-deposition substrate anneal applied to Wang_2026 transmon devices intended to recrystallize the sapphire substrate, and if so, does it also recrystallize or grain-grow the Re film in a way that would override any deposition-temperature-set microstructure? This is critical for determining whether deposition temperature is even a meaningful independent variable for Tc in this fabrication flow.

Are there unpublished or in-preparation Re deposition studies (from the Wang group or others) that systematically vary deposition temperature and measure Tc, which could be added to the corpus to make this hypothesis testable?

Given that bulk Re Tc is ~1.69–1.97 K and the observed value is 1.9 K, should the QREM layer treat Re Tc as effectively fixed at the bulk value for DC magnetron sputtering at high temperature, pending evidence of a tunable regime?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next step is to identify or commission a systematic Re deposition study varying substrate temperature from ~500°C to 1000°C (in ~100°C increments) on the same substrate type, with Tc measured by four-probe transport for each condition and post-deposition annealing either omitted or held constant. Pairing Tc measurements with XRD (to track crystallographic phase and grain size) and residual resistivity ratio (RRR) would allow the microstructural mechanism linking deposition temperature to Tc to be identified, enabling a physically grounded QREM mapping function rather than a purely empirical fit.
